title: Cary Grant

infobox.value: Archibald Alec Leach  (1904-01-18)18 January 1904  [Horfield](/wiki/Horfield "Horfield"), Bristol, England

* [Virginia Cherrill](/wiki/Virginia_Cherrill "Virginia Cherrill")  ​  ​ (m. 1934; div. 1935)​ * [Barbara Hutton](/wiki/Barbara_Hutton "Barbara Hutton")  ​  ​ (m. 1942; div. 1945)​ * [Betsy Drake](/wiki/Betsy_Drake "Betsy Drake")  ​  ​ (m. 1949; div. 1962)​ * [Dyan Cannon](/wiki/Dyan_Cannon "Dyan Cannon")  ​  ​ (m. 1965; div. 1968)​ * Barbara Harris  ​ (m. 1981)​
* [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") (1970) * [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors") (1981)

(born **Archibald Alec Leach**;[[a]](#cite_note-MiddleName-4) 18 January 1904 – 29 November 1986) was an English-American actor. Known for his blended British and American accent, debonair demeanor, lighthearted approach to acting, and sense of comic timing, he was one of classic Hollywood's definitive leading men. He was nominated twice for the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor"), received an [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") in [1970](/wiki/42nd_Academy_Awards "42nd Academy Awards"), and received the [Kennedy Center Honor](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honor "Kennedy Center Honor") in 1981.[[4]](#cite_note-5) He was named the [second-greatest male star](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Stars#List_of_50_greatest_screen_legends:_Top_25_Male_and_Top_25_Female_stars "AFI's 100 Years...100 Stars") of the [Golden Age of Hollywood](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Hollywood "Golden Age of Hollywood") by the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") in 1999.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

**Cary Grant** (born **Archibald Alec Leach**;[[a]](#cite_note-MiddleName-4) 18 January 1904 – 29 November 1986) was an English-American actor. Known for his blended British and American accent, debonair demeanor, lighthearted approach to acting, and sense of comic timing, he was one of classic Hollywood's definitive leading men. He was nominated twice for the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor"), received an [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") in [1970](/wiki/42nd_Academy_Awards "42nd Academy Awards"), and received the [Kennedy Center Honor](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honor "Kennedy Center Honor") in 1981.[[4]](#cite_note-5) He was named the [second-greatest male star](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Stars#List_of_50_greatest_screen_legends:_Top_25_Male_and_Top_25_Female_stars "AFI's 100 Years...100 Stars") of the [Golden Age of Hollywood](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Hollywood "Golden Age of Hollywood") by the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") in 1999.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Grant was born into an impoverished family in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), where he had an unhappy childhood marked by the absence of his mother and his father's alcoholism. He became attracted to theatre at a young age when he visited the [Bristol Hippodrome](/wiki/Bristol_Hippodrome "Bristol Hippodrome").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199735Nelson200210-7) At 16, he went as a stage performer with the Pender Troupe for a tour of the US. After a series of successful performances in New York City, he decided to stay there.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199744–46-8) He established a name for himself in [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") in the 1920s and toured the United States before moving to Hollywood in the early 1930s.

Grant initially appeared in crime films and dramas, such as *[Blonde Venus](/wiki/Blonde_Venus "Blonde Venus")* (1932) and *[She Done Him Wrong](/wiki/She_Done_Him_Wrong "She Done Him Wrong")* (1933), but later gained renown for his performances in romantic [screwball](/wiki/Screwball_comedy "Screwball comedy") comedies such as *[The Awful Truth](/wiki/The_Awful_Truth "The Awful Truth")* (1937), *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")* (1938), *[His Girl Friday](/wiki/His_Girl_Friday "His Girl Friday")* (1940), and *[The Philadelphia Story](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Story_(film) "The Philadelphia Story (film)")* (1940). These pictures are frequently cited among the greatest comedy films of all time.[[8]](#cite_note-ATCF-9) Other well-known films in which he starred in this period were the adventure *[Gunga Din](/wiki/Gunga_Din_(film) "Gunga Din (film)")* (1939), the [dark comedy](/wiki/Dark_comedy "Dark comedy") *[Arsenic and Old Lace](/wiki/Arsenic_and_Old_Lace_(film) "Arsenic and Old Lace (film)")* (1944), and the dramas *[Only Angels Have Wings](/wiki/Only_Angels_Have_Wings "Only Angels Have Wings")* (1939), *[Penny Serenade](/wiki/Penny_Serenade "Penny Serenade")* (1941), and *[None but the Lonely Heart](/wiki/None_but_the_Lonely_Heart_(film) "None but the Lonely Heart (film)")* (1944), the latter two for which he was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor").

During the 1940s and 1950s, Grant had a close working relationship with director [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), who cast him in four films: *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941), *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946), *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")* (1955), and *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959). For the suspense-dramas *Suspicion* and *Notorious*, Grant took on darker, morally ambiguous characters, both challenging Grant's screen persona and his acting abilities. Toward the end of his career he starred in the romantic films *[An Affair to Remember](/wiki/An_Affair_to_Remember "An Affair to Remember")* (1957), *[Indiscreet](/wiki/Indiscreet_(1958_film) "Indiscreet (1958 film)")* (1958), *[Operation Petticoat](/wiki/Operation_Petticoat "Operation Petticoat")* (1959), *[That Touch of Mink](/wiki/That_Touch_of_Mink "That Touch of Mink")* (1962), and *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")* (1963). He is remembered by critics for his unusually broad appeal as a handsome, suave actor who did not take himself too seriously, and in comedies was able to toy with his dignity without sacrificing it entirely.

Grant was married five times, three of them elopements, with actresses [Virginia Cherrill](/wiki/Virginia_Cherrill "Virginia Cherrill") (1934–1935), [Betsy Drake](/wiki/Betsy_Drake "Betsy Drake") (1949–1962), and [Dyan Cannon](/wiki/Dyan_Cannon "Dyan Cannon") (1965–1968). He had a daughter, [Jennifer Grant](/wiki/Jennifer_Grant "Jennifer Grant"), with Cannon. He retired from film acting in 1966 and pursued numerous business interests, representing cosmetics firm [Fabergé](/wiki/Faberg%C3%A9_(cosmetics) "Fabergé (cosmetics)") and sitting on the board of [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"). He died of a stroke in 1986 at the age of 82.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Grant was born Archibald Alec Leach at 15 Hughenden Road in [Horfield](/wiki/Horfield "Horfield"), a suburb of [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), England.[[2]](#cite_note-BirthRecordList-2)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199713Eliot2004390-10) He was the second child of Elias James Leach and Elsie Maria Leach (née Kingdon).[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201113-11) His father worked as a tailor's presser at a clothes factory, while his mother worked as a seamstress.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200424-12) His older brother John William Elias Leach died of [tuberculous meningitis](/wiki/Tuberculous_meningitis "Tuberculous meningitis") two days before his first birthday.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200425-13) Grant may have considered himself partly [Jewish](/wiki/English_Jews "English Jews") but was raised Anglican from his mother's background.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[b]](#cite_note-19) He had an unhappy upbringing; his father was an alcoholic[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlein200932-20) and his mother had clinical depression.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeiten1996291-21)

> He had such a traumatic childhood, it was horrible. I work with a lot of kids on the street and I've heard a lot of stories about what happens when a family breaks down — but his was just horrendous.

—Grant's wife Dyan Cannon on his childhood[[20]](#cite_note-SMH-22)

Grant's mother taught him song and dance when he was four, and she was keen on him having piano lessons.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201114-23) She occasionally took him to the cinema, where he enjoyed the performances of [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [Chester Conklin](/wiki/Chester_Conklin "Chester Conklin"), [Fatty Arbuckle](/wiki/Fatty_Arbuckle "Fatty Arbuckle"), [Ford Sterling](/wiki/Ford_Sterling "Ford Sterling"), [Mack Swain](/wiki/Mack_Swain "Mack Swain"), and [Broncho Billy Anderson](/wiki/Broncho_Billy_Anderson "Broncho Billy Anderson").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199720-24) He moved a short distance to 50 Berkeley Road at about age 4,[[23]](#cite_note-bp-20241122-25) and was sent to [Bishop Road Primary School](/wiki/Bishop_Road_Primary_School "Bishop Road Primary School") when he was 4+1⁄2.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell198332-26) He lived at six addresses in Bristol, but said his time at Berkeley Road were his childhood's "happiest days".[[25]](#cite_note-guardian-20241122-27)

Grant's biographer [Graham McCann](/wiki/Graham_McCann "Graham McCann") claimed that his mother "did not know how to give affection and did not know how to receive it either".[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199727-28) Biographer [Geoffrey Wansell](/wiki/Geoffrey_Wansell "Geoffrey Wansell") notes that his mother blamed herself bitterly for the death of Grant's brother John, and never recovered from it.[[c]](#cite_note-29) Grant acknowledged that his negative experiences with his mother affected his relationships with women later in life.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling200163-30) She frowned on alcohol and tobacco,[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201113-11) and reduced his pocket money for minor mishaps.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199719-31) Grant attributed her behavior to overprotectiveness, fearing that she would lose him as she did John.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201114-23)

When Grant was nine, his father placed his mother in [Glenside Hospital](/wiki/Glenside,_Bristol "Glenside, Bristol"), a mental institution, and told him she had gone away on a "long holiday",[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197313-32) later declaring that she had died.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlein200932-20) Grant grew up resenting his mother, particularly after being told she left the family. After she was institutionalised, Grant and his father moved into Grant's grandmother's home in Bristol.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyceDonaldson1989298Nelson200236-33) When Grant was ten, his father remarried and started a new family.[[20]](#cite_note-SMH-22) Grant did not learn that his mother was still alive until he was 31,[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly2014209-34) his father confessing to the lie shortly before his own death.[[20]](#cite_note-SMH-22) Grant made arrangements for his mother to leave the institution in June 1935, shortly after he learned of her whereabouts.[[32]](#cite_note-35) He visited her regularly,[[23]](#cite_note-bp-20241122-25) including after filming *Gunga Din* in October 1938.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201194-36)

Grant enjoyed the theater, particularly pantomimes at Christmas, which he attended with his father.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199719-31) He befriended a troupe of acrobatic dancers known as The Penders or the Bob Pender Stage Troupe.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERood1994140-37) He subsequently trained as a stilt walker and began touring with them.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERood1994140Miniter2013194-38) [Jesse Lasky](/wiki/Jesse_Lasky "Jesse Lasky") was a Broadway producer at the time and saw Grant performing at the [Wintergarten theater](/wiki/Berlin_Wintergarten_theatre "Berlin Wintergarten theatre") in Berlin around 1914.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFryer2005164Louvish200740Miniter2013194-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Fairfield_School%2C_Montpelier%2C_Bristol._-_geograph.org.uk_-_198016.jpg/250px-Fairfield_School%2C_Montpelier%2C_Bristol._-_geograph.org.uk_-_198016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fairfield_School,_Montpelier,_Bristol._-_geograph.org.uk_-_198016.jpg)

[Fairfield Grammar School](/wiki/Fairfield_Grammar_School "Fairfield Grammar School"), which Grant attended between 1915 and 1918

In 1915, Grant won a scholarship to attend [Fairfield Grammar School](/wiki/Fairfield_Grammar_School "Fairfield Grammar School") in Bristol, although his father could barely afford to pay for the uniform.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199729-40) He was quite capable in most academic subjects,[[d]](#cite_note-42) but he excelled at sports, particularly [fives](/wiki/Fives "Fives"), and his good looks and acrobatic talents made him a popular figure.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199733-43)[[40]](#cite_note-44) He developed a reputation for mischief, and frequently refused to do his homework.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199730-45) A former classmate referred to him as a "scruffy little boy", while an old teacher remembered "the naughty little boy who was always making a noise in the back row and would never do his homework".[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199733-43)

He spent his evenings working backstage in Bristol theatres, and at the age of 13, was responsible for the lighting for magician [David Devant](/wiki/David_Devant "David Devant") at the Bristol Empire in 1917.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling200121-46) He began hanging around backstage at the theatre at every opportunity,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201116-41) and volunteered for work in the summer as a messenger boy and guide at the military docks in [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), to escape the unhappiness of his home life.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199734-47) The time spent at Southampton strengthened his desire to travel; he was eager to leave Bristol and tried to sign on as a ship's cabin boy, but he was too young.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199730–31-48)

On 13 March 1918, the 14-year-old[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199737-49) Grant was expelled from Fairfield.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFells2015105-50) Several explanations were given, including being discovered in the girls' lavatory[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel200929-51) and assisting two other classmates with theft in the nearby town of [Almondsbury](/wiki/Almondsbury "Almondsbury").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199737–38-52) Wansell claims that Grant had set out intentionally to get himself expelled from school to pursue a career in entertainment with the troupe,[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201117-53) and he did rejoin Pender's troupe three days after being expelled. His father had a better-paying job in Southampton, and Grant's expulsion brought local authorities to Pender's door with questions about why he was living in Bristol and not with his father in Southampton. His father then co-signed a three-year contract between Grant and Pender that stipulated Grant's weekly salary, along with room and board, dancing lessons, and other training for his profession until age 18. There was also a provision in the contract for salary raises based on job performance.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199734Nelson200242Eliot200434-54)

## Vaudeville and performing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Vaudeville and performing career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Hippodrome_interior.jpg/250px-Hippodrome_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hippodrome_interior.jpg)

The [New York Hippodrome](/wiki/New_York_Hippodrome "New York Hippodrome") where Grant performed

The Pender Troupe began touring the country, and Grant's performing pantomime developed his physical skills, broadening the range of his acting.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201117-53) The troupe traveled on the [RMS *Olympic*](/wiki/RMS_Olympic "RMS Olympic") to conduct a tour of the United States on 21 July 1920, when he was 16, arriving a week later.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199744–46-8) Biographer [Richard Schickel](/wiki/Richard_Schickel "Richard Schickel") writes that [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") and [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") were aboard the same ship, returning from their honeymoon; Grant played shuffleboard with Fairbanks, who became an important role model for him.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel199820-55) After arriving in New York, the group performed at the [New York Hippodrome](/wiki/New_York_Hippodrome "New York Hippodrome"), the largest theater in the world at the time with a capacity of 5,697. They performed there for nine months, putting on 12 shows a week, and they had a successful production of *[Good Times](/wiki/Good_Times_(musical) "Good Times (musical)")*.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199744–46Wansell201117-56)

> Doing stand-up comedy is extremely difficult. Your timing has to change from show to show and from town to town. You're always adjusting to the size of the audience and the size of the theatre.

—Grant on stand-up comedy.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199753-57)

Grant became a part of the [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") circuit and began touring, performing in places such as [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), and [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"),[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201118-58) and he decided to stay in the US with several of the other members when the rest of the troupe returned to Britain.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2014100-59) He became fond of the [Marx Brothers](/wiki/Marx_Brothers "Marx Brothers") during this period, and [Zeppo Marx](/wiki/Zeppo_Marx "Zeppo Marx") was an early role model for him.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199749-60) In July 1922, he performed in a group called the "Knockabout Comedians" at the [Palace Theater](/wiki/Palace_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Palace Theatre (New York City)") on Broadway.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201118-58) He formed another group that summer called "The Walking Stanleys" with several of the former members of the Pender Troupe, and he starred in a variety show named "Better Times" at the Hippodrome towards the end of the year.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199751Wansell201118-61) While serving as a paid escort for the opera singer [Lucrezia Bori](/wiki/Lucrezia_Bori "Lucrezia Bori") at a Park Avenue party, he met [George C. Tilyou Jr.](/wiki/George_C._Tilyou#Personal_life "George C. Tilyou"), whose family owned [Steeplechase Park](/wiki/Steeplechase_Park "Steeplechase Park").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201118-58) Learning of his acrobatic experience, Tilyou hired him to work as a stilt-walker and attract large crowds on the newly opened [Coney Island Boardwalk](/wiki/Coney_Island_Boardwalk "Coney Island Boardwalk"), wearing a bright [greatcoat](/wiki/Greatcoat "Greatcoat") and a [sandwich board](/wiki/Sandwich_board "Sandwich board") that advertised the amusement park.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199749-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Casino_Theatre%2C_Broadway_and_39th_Street%2C_Manhattan_-_crop.jpg/250px-Casino_Theatre%2C_Broadway_and_39th_Street%2C_Manhattan_-_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casino_Theatre,_Broadway_and_39th_Street,_Manhattan_-_crop.jpg)

The [Casino Theater](/wiki/Casino_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Casino Theatre (New York City)") on Broadway and 39th Street, where Grant appeared in Shubert's *Boom-Boom*

Grant spent the next couple of years touring the United States with "The Walking Stanleys". He visited Los Angeles for the first time in 1924, which made a lasting impression on him.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201118-58) The group split up and he returned to New York, where he began performing at the [National Vaudeville Artists](/wiki/National_Vaudeville_Artists "National Vaudeville Artists") Club on West 46th Street, doing comic sketches, juggling, performing acrobatics, and as "Rubber Legs", riding a unicycle.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199751-62) The experience was a particularly demanding one, but it gave Grant the opportunity to improve his comic technique and to develop skills that benefitted him later in Hollywood.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199753Roberts2014100-63)

Grant became a leading man alongside [Jean Dalrymple](/wiki/Jean_Dalrymple "Jean Dalrymple") and decided to form the "Jack Janis Company", which began touring vaudeville.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlide2012211-64) He was sometimes mistaken for an Australian during this period and was nicknamed "Kangaroo" or "Boomerang".[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201118–19-65) His accent seemed to have changed as a result of moving to London with the Pender troupe and working in so many music halls in the UK and the US, eventually developing a sort of [transatlantic or mid-Atlantic accent](/wiki/Good_American_Speech "Good American Speech").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199759–60Walker2015187-66)[[e]](#cite_note-69) In 1927, he was cast as an Australian in Reggie Hammerstein's musical *Golden Dawn*, for which he earned $75 a week.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201119-70) The show was not well received, but it lasted for 184 performances and several critics started to notice Grant as the "pleasant new juvenile" or "competent young newcomer".[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201119-70)

In 1928, he joined the [William Morris Agency](/wiki/William_Morris_Agency "William Morris Agency") and was offered another juvenile part by Hammerstein in his play *Polly*, an unsuccessful production.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonnelley2003290Wansell201119-71) One critic wrote that Grant "has a strong masculine manner, but unfortunately fails to bring out the beauty of the score".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199753-57) Wansell notes that the pressure of a failing production began to make him fret, and he was eventually dropped from the run after six weeks of poor reviews.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201120-72) Despite the setback, Hammerstein's rival [Florenz Ziegfeld](/wiki/Florenz_Ziegfeld "Florenz Ziegfeld") made an attempt to buy Grant's contract, but Hammerstein sold it to the [Shubert Brothers](/wiki/Shubert_Brothers "Shubert Brothers") instead.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201120-72)

[J. J. Shubert](/wiki/J._J._Shubert "J. J. Shubert") cast him in a small role as a Spaniard opposite [Jeanette MacDonald](/wiki/Jeanette_MacDonald "Jeanette MacDonald") in the French risqué comedy *Boom-Boom* at the Casino Theater on Broadway, which premiered on 28 January 1929, ten days after his 25th birthday.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell198375Turk1998350-73) MacDonald later admitted that Grant was "absolutely terrible in the role", but he exhibited a charm that endeared him to people and effectively saved the show from failure.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201120-72) The play ran for 72 shows, and Grant earned $350 a week before moving to Detroit, and then to Chicago.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199754Wansell201120-74)[[f]](#cite_note-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Cary_Grant_circa_1930.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_circa_1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_circa_1930.jpg)

Grant in 1930

To console himself, Grant bought a 1927 [Packard](/wiki/Packard "Packard") sport [phaeton](/wiki/Phaeton_body "Phaeton body").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201120-72) He visited his half-brother Eric in England, and he returned to New York to play the role of Max Grunewald in a Shubert production of *A Wonderful Night*.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETraubner2004115-76) It premiered at the Majestic Theatre on 31 October 1929, two days after the [Wall Street Crash](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash "Wall Street Crash"), and lasted until February 1930 with 125 shows.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199755Wansell201120-77) The play received mixed reviews; one critic criticized his acting, likening it to a "mixture of [John Barrymore](/wiki/John_Barrymore "John Barrymore") and [cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney")", while another announced that he had brought a "breath of elfin Broadway" to the role.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199755-78) Grant found it difficult forming relationships with women, remarking that he "never seemed able to fully communicate with them" even after many years "surrounded by all sorts of attractive girls" in the theater, on the road, and in New York.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201121-79)

In 1930, Grant toured for nine months in a production of the musical *The Street Singer*.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199756-80) It ended in early 1931, and the Shuberts invited him to spend the summer performing on the stage at [The Muny](/wiki/The_Muny "The Muny") in [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"); he appeared in 12 different productions, putting on 87 shows.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner19736-81)[[g]](#cite_note-83) He received praise from local newspapers for these performances, gaining a reputation as a romantic leading man.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199756-80) Significant influences on his acting in this period were [Gerald du Maurier](/wiki/Gerald_du_Maurier "Gerald du Maurier"), [A. E. Matthews](/wiki/A._E._Matthews "A. E. Matthews"), [Jack Buchanan](/wiki/Jack_Buchanan "Jack Buchanan"), and [Ronald Squire](/wiki/Ronald_Squire "Ronald Squire").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199771-84) He admitted that he was drawn to acting because of a "great need to be liked and admired".[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201113-11)

He was eventually fired by the Shuberts at the end of the summer season when he refused to accept a pay cut because of financial difficulties caused by [the Depression](/wiki/The_Depression "The Depression").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201121-79) His unemployment was short-lived, however; impresario [William B. Friedlander](/wiki/William_B._Friedlander "William B. Friedlander") offered him the romantic lead in his musical *Nikki*, and Grant starred opposite [Fay Wray](/wiki/Fay_Wray "Fay Wray") as a soldier in post-World War I France. The production opened on 29 September 1931, in New York, but was stopped after just 39 performances due to the effects of the Depression.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201121-79)

## Film career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Film career")]

See also: [List of Cary Grant performances](/wiki/List_of_Cary_Grant_performances "List of Cary Grant performances")

### 1932–1936: Debut and early roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1932–1936: Debut and early roles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/This_is_the_Night.jpg/250px-This_is_the_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:This_is_the_Night.jpg)

With [Roland Young](/wiki/Roland_Young "Roland Young") (*right*), [Lili Damita](/wiki/Lili_Damita "Lili Damita") (*center*), and [Charlie Ruggles](/wiki/Charlie_Ruggles "Charlie Ruggles") (*far left*) in his debut film *[This is the Night](/wiki/This_Is_the_Night_(1932_film) "This Is the Night (1932 film)")* (1932)

Grant's role in *Nikki* was praised by [Ed Sullivan](/wiki/Ed_Sullivan "Ed Sullivan") of *[The New York Daily News](/wiki/The_New_York_Daily_News "The New York Daily News")*, who noted that the "young lad from England" had "a big future in the movies".[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200454–55-85) The review led to another screen test by Paramount Publix, resulting in an appearance as a sailor in *[Singapore Sue](/wiki/Singapore_Sue "Singapore Sue")* (1931),[[79]](#cite_note-86) a ten-minute short film by [Casey Robinson](/wiki/Casey_Robinson "Casey Robinson").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200454–55-85) Grant delivered his lines "without any conviction" according to McCann.[[h]](#cite_note-88) Through Robinson, Grant met with [Jesse L. Lasky](/wiki/Jesse_L._Lasky "Jesse L. Lasky") and [B. P. Schulberg](/wiki/B._P._Schulberg "B. P. Schulberg"), the co-founder and general manager of [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), respectively.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200456–57-89) After a successful screen-test directed by [Marion Gering](/wiki/Marion_Gering "Marion Gering"),[[i]](#cite_note-90) Schulberg signed a contract with the 27-year-old Grant on 7 December 1931, for five years,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199762-91) at a starting salary of $450 a week.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye1973Wansell201121-92) Schulberg demanded that he change his name to "something that sounded more all-American like [Gary Cooper](/wiki/Gary_Cooper "Gary Cooper")", and they eventually agreed on Cary Grant.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200457-93)[[j]](#cite_note-95)

Grant set out to establish himself as what McCann calls the "epitome of masculine glamour", and made Douglas Fairbanks his first role model.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199765-96) McCann notes that Grant's career in Hollywood immediately took off because he exhibited a "genuine charm", which made him stand out among the other good looking actors at the time, making it "remarkably easy to find people who were willing to support his embryonic career".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199760-97) He made his feature film debut with the [Frank Tuttle](/wiki/Frank_Tuttle "Frank Tuttle")-directed comedy *[This is the Night](/wiki/This_Is_the_Night_(1932_film) "This Is the Night (1932 film)")* (1932), playing an Olympic javelin thrower opposite [Thelma Todd](/wiki/Thelma_Todd "Thelma Todd") and [Lili Damita](/wiki/Lili_Damita "Lili Damita").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197320Eliot200462-98) Grant disliked his role and threatened to leave Hollywood,[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200462Wansell201122-99) but to his surprise a critic from *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* praised his performance, and thought that he looked like a "potential femme rave".[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200463-100)

In 1932, Grant played a wealthy [playboy](/wiki/Playboy_lifestyle "Playboy lifestyle") opposite [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich") in *[Blonde Venus](/wiki/Blonde_Venus "Blonde Venus")*, directed by [Josef von Sternberg](/wiki/Josef_von_Sternberg "Josef von Sternberg"). Grant's role is described by [William Rothman](/wiki/William_Rothman "William Rothman") as projecting the "distinctive kind of nonmacho masculinity that was to enable him to incarnate a man capable of being a romantic hero".[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothman201471-101) Grant found that he conflicted with the director during the filming and the two often argued in German.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199780-102) He played a suave playboy type in a number of films: *[Merrily We Go to Hell](/wiki/Merrily_We_Go_to_Hell "Merrily We Go to Hell")* opposite [Fredric March](/wiki/Fredric_March "Fredric March") and [Sylvia Sidney](/wiki/Sylvia_Sidney "Sylvia Sidney"), *[Devil and the Deep](/wiki/Devil_and_the_Deep "Devil and the Deep")* with [Tallulah Bankhead](/wiki/Tallulah_Bankhead "Tallulah Bankhead"), [Gary Cooper](/wiki/Gary_Cooper "Gary Cooper") and [Charles Laughton](/wiki/Charles_Laughton "Charles Laughton") (Cooper and Grant had no scenes together), *[Hot Saturday](/wiki/Hot_Saturday "Hot Saturday")* opposite [Nancy Carroll](/wiki/Nancy_Carroll "Nancy Carroll") and [Randolph Scott](/wiki/Randolph_Scott "Randolph Scott"),[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201129-103) and *[Madame Butterfly](/wiki/Madame_Butterfly_(1932_film) "Madame Butterfly (1932 film)")* with Sidney.[[94]](#cite_note-Filmography-104)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200463–68-105) According to biographer Marc Eliot, while these films did not make Grant a star, they did well enough to establish him as one of Hollywood's "new crop of fast-rising actors".[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200466-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Grant_West_I%27m_No_Angel_Still_1933.jpg/250px-Grant_West_I%27m_No_Angel_Still_1933.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grant_West_I%27m_No_Angel_Still_1933.jpg)

Grant and [Mae West](/wiki/Mae_West "Mae West") in *[I'm No Angel](/wiki/I%27m_No_Angel "I'm No Angel")* (1933)

In 1933, Grant gained attention for appearing in the [pre-Code films](/wiki/Pre-Code_Hollywood "Pre-Code Hollywood") *[She Done Him Wrong](/wiki/She_Done_Him_Wrong "She Done Him Wrong")* and *[I'm No Angel](/wiki/I%27m_No_Angel "I'm No Angel")* opposite [Mae West](/wiki/Mae_West "Mae West").[[k]](#cite_note-109) West later claimed that she had discovered Cary Grant.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201130-110)[[l]](#cite_note-112) Of course Grant had already made *Blonde Venus* the previous year in which he was Marlene Dietrich's [leading man](/wiki/Leading_man "Leading man"). [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") noted that Grant did not appear confident in his role as a [Salvation Army](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") director in *She Done Him Wrong*, which made it all the more charming.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199786-113)[[102]](#cite_note-Kael-114) The film was a box office hit, earning more than $2 million in the United States,[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201131-115) and has since won much acclaim.[[m]](#cite_note-118) For *I'm No Angel*, Grant's salary was increased from $450 to $750 a week.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200473-119) The film was even more successful than *She Done Him Wrong*, and saved Paramount from bankruptcy;[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200473-119) Vermilye cites it as one of the best comedy films of the 1930s.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197337–38Eliot200491-120)

A string of financially unsuccessful films followed, including roles as a president of a company who is sued for knocking down a boy in an accident in *[Born to Be Bad](/wiki/Born_to_Be_Bad_(1934_film) "Born to Be Bad (1934 film)")* (1934) for 20th Century Fox,[[n]](#cite_note-122) a cosmetic surgeon in *[Kiss and Make-Up](/wiki/Kiss_and_Make-Up "Kiss and Make-Up")* (1934),[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalliwell197623-123) and a blinded pilot opposite [Myrna Loy](/wiki/Myrna_Loy "Myrna Loy") in *[Wings in the Dark](/wiki/Wings_in_the_Dark "Wings in the Dark")* (1935). Amid press reports of problems in his marriage to Cherrill,[[o]](#cite_note-125) Paramount concluded that Grant was expendable.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201138-124)[[p]](#cite_note-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Ladies_Should_Listen_2.jpg/250px-Ladies_Should_Listen_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ladies_Should_Listen_2.jpg)

Lobby card for *[Ladies Should Listen](/wiki/Ladies_Should_Listen "Ladies Should Listen")* (1934) with [Frances Drake](/wiki/Frances_Drake "Frances Drake") and Grant

Grant's prospects picked up in the latter half of 1935 when he was loaned out to [RKO Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197348-129) Producer [Pandro Berman](/wiki/Pandro_Berman "Pandro Berman") agreed to take him on in the face of failure because "I'd seen him do things which were excellent, and [[Katharine] Hepburn](/wiki/Katharine_Hepburn "Katharine Hepburn") wanted him too."[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201139-130) His first venture with RKO, playing a raffish Cockney swindler in [George Cukor](/wiki/George_Cukor "George Cukor")'s *[Sylvia Scarlett](/wiki/Sylvia_Scarlett "Sylvia Scarlett")* (1935), was the first of four collaborations with Hepburn.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197348–49Deschner197388–89-131)[[q]](#cite_note-133) Though a commercial failure,[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel199846-134) his dominating performance was praised by critics,[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197348–49Wansell201141-135) and Grant always considered the film to have been the breakthrough for his career.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199789-136)

When his contract with Paramount ended in 1936 with the release of *[Wedding Present](/wiki/Wedding_Present_(film) "Wedding Present (film)")*, Grant decided not to renew it and wished to work freelance. Grant claimed to be the first freelance actor in Hollywood.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197355-137) His first venture as a freelance actor was *[The Amazing Quest of Ernest Bliss](/wiki/The_Amazing_Quest_of_Ernest_Bliss "The Amazing Quest of Ernest Bliss")* (1936), which was shot in England.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199789-136) The film was a [box office bomb](/wiki/Box_office_bomb "Box office bomb") and prompted Grant to reconsider his decision. Critical and commercial success with *[Suzy](/wiki/Suzy_(film) "Suzy (film)")* later that year in which he played a French airman opposite [Jean Harlow](/wiki/Jean_Harlow "Jean Harlow") and [Franchot Tone](/wiki/Franchot_Tone "Franchot Tone"), led to him signing joint contracts with RKO and [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures"), enabling him to choose the stories that he felt suited his acting style.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197355-137) His Columbia contract was a four-film deal over two years, guaranteeing him $50,000 each for the first two and $75,000 each for the others.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201142-138)

### 1937–1945: Hollywood stardom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1937–1945: Hollywood stardom")]

In 1937, Grant began the first film under his contract with Columbia Pictures, *[When You're in Love](/wiki/When_You%27re_in_Love_(film) "When You're in Love (film)")*, portraying a wealthy American artist who eventually woos a famous opera singer ([Grace Moore](/wiki/Grace_Moore "Grace Moore")). His performance received positive feedback from critics, with Mae Tinee of *[The Chicago Daily Tribune](/wiki/The_Chicago_Daily_Tribune "The Chicago Daily Tribune")* describing it as the "best thing he's done in a long time".[[122]](#cite_note-139) After a commercial failure in his second RKO venture *[The Toast of New York](/wiki/The_Toast_of_New_York "The Toast of New York")*,[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201143-140)[[124]](#cite_note-rko-141) Grant was loaned to [Hal Roach](/wiki/Hal_Roach "Hal Roach")'s studio for *[Topper](/wiki/Topper_(film) "Topper (film)")*, a [screwball comedy](/wiki/Screwball_comedy "Screwball comedy") film distributed by [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM"), which became his first major comedy success.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197358-142) Grant played one half of a wealthy, freewheeling married couple with [Constance Bennett](/wiki/Constance_Bennett "Constance Bennett"),[[126]](#cite_note-143) who wreak havoc on the world as ghosts after dying in a car accident.[[127]](#cite_note-144) *Topper* became one of the most popular movies of the year, with a critic from *Variety* noting that both Grant and Bennett "do their assignments with great skill".[[128]](#cite_note-145) Vermilye described the film's success as "a logical springboard" for Grant to star in *[The Awful Truth](/wiki/The_Awful_Truth "The Awful Truth")* that year,[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197360-146) his first film made with [Irene Dunne](/wiki/Irene_Dunne "Irene Dunne") and [Ralph Bellamy](/wiki/Ralph_Bellamy "Ralph Bellamy"). Though director [Leo McCarey](/wiki/Leo_McCarey "Leo McCarey") reportedly disliked Grant,[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201148–49-147) who had mocked the director by enacting his mannerisms in the film,[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehring2005152-148) he recognized Grant's comic talents and encouraged him to improvise his lines and draw upon his skills developed in vaudeville.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201148–49-147) The film was a critical and commercial success and made Grant a top Hollywood star,[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197361HighamMoseley1990103-149) establishing a screen persona for him as a sophisticated light comedy leading man in screwball comedies.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehring2002115-150)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Bringing_up_baby_film_still.jpg/250px-Bringing_up_baby_film_still.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bringing_up_baby_film_still.jpg)

[Katharine Hepburn](/wiki/Katharine_Hepburn "Katharine Hepburn") and Grant in *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")* (1938)

*The Awful Truth* began what film critic [Benjamin Schwarz](/wiki/Benjamin_Schwarz_(writer) "Benjamin Schwarz (writer)") of *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* later called "the most spectacular run ever for an actor in American pictures" for Grant.[[134]](#cite_note-schwarz20070102-151) In 1938, he starred opposite [Katharine Hepburn](/wiki/Katharine_Hepburn "Katharine Hepburn") in the screwball comedy *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")*, featuring a [leopard](/wiki/Leopard "Leopard") and frequent bickering and verbal jousting between Grant and Hepburn.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMast1988265KarnickJenkins2013330-152) He was initially uncertain how to play his character, but was told by director [Howard Hawks](/wiki/Howard_Hawks "Howard Hawks") to think of [Harold Lloyd](/wiki/Harold_Lloyd "Harold Lloyd").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMast1988294-153) Grant was given more leeway in the comic scenes, the editing of the film and in educating Hepburn in the art of comedy.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997115-154) Despite losing over $350,000 for RKO,[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201152-155) the film earned rave reviews from critics.[[139]](#cite_note-156) He again appeared with Hepburn in the romantic comedy *[Holiday](/wiki/Holiday_(1938_film) "Holiday (1938 film)")* later that year, which did not fare well commercially, to the point that Hepburn was considered to be "box office poison" at the time.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehring2002123-157)

Despite a series of commercial failures, Grant was now more popular than ever and in high demand.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201153-158) According to Vermilye, in 1939, Grant played roles that were more dramatic, albeit with comical undertones.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197367-159) He played a British army sergeant opposite [Douglas Fairbanks Jr.](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks_Jr. "Douglas Fairbanks Jr.") in the [George Stevens](/wiki/George_Stevens "George Stevens")-directed adventure film *[Gunga Din](/wiki/Gunga_Din_(film) "Gunga Din (film)")*, set at a military station in [India](/wiki/British_India "British India").[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201153MintzRobertsWelky2016144-160)[[r]](#cite_note-162) Roles as a pilot opposite [Jean Arthur](/wiki/Jean_Arthur "Jean Arthur") and [Rita Hayworth](/wiki/Rita_Hayworth "Rita Hayworth") in Hawks' *[Only Angels Have Wings](/wiki/Only_Angels_Have_Wings "Only Angels Have Wings")*,[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201155-163) and a wealthy landowner alongside [Carole Lombard](/wiki/Carole_Lombard "Carole Lombard") in *[In Name Only](/wiki/In_Name_Only "In Name Only")* followed.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehring2003188-164)

In 1940, Grant played a callous newspaper editor who learns that his ex-wife and former journalist, played by [Rosalind Russell](/wiki/Rosalind_Russell "Rosalind Russell"), is to marry insurance officer Ralph Bellamy in Hawks' comedy *[His Girl Friday](/wiki/His_Girl_Friday "His Girl Friday")*,[[147]](#cite_note-165) which was praised for its strong chemistry and "great verbal athleticism" between Grant and Russell.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201159–60-166)[[149]](#cite_note-Guardian_HGF-167)[[s]](#cite_note-168) Grant reunited with Irene Dunne in *[My Favorite Wife](/wiki/My_Favorite_Wife "My Favorite Wife")*, a "first rate comedy" according to *[Life](/wiki/Life_(magazine) "Life (magazine)")* magazine,[[150]](#cite_note-169) which became RKO's second biggest picture of the year, with profits of $505,000.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJewellHarbin198255-170)[[t]](#cite_note-172) After playing a Virginian backwoodsman in *[The Howards of Virginia](/wiki/The_Howards_of_Virginia "The Howards of Virginia")*, set during the American Revolution – which McCann considers to have been Grant's worst film and performance[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997173-173) – his last film of the year was in the critically lauded romantic comedy *[The Philadelphia Story](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Story_(film) "The Philadelphia Story (film)")*, in which he played the ex-husband of Hepburn's character.[[154]](#cite_note-174)[[155]](#cite_note-175)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001124-176) Grant felt his performance was so strong that he was bitterly disappointed not to have received an Oscar nomination, especially since both his lead co-stars, Hepburn and [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart"), received them, with Stewart winning for Best Actor.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001133,_135-177) Grant joked "I'd have to blacken my teeth first before the Academy will take me seriously".[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001133,_135-177) Film historian David Thomson wrote that "the wrong man got the Oscar" for *The Philadelphia Story* and that "Grant got better performances out of Hepburn than (her long-time companion) [Spencer Tracy](/wiki/Spencer_Tracy "Spencer Tracy") ever managed."[[158]](#cite_note-David_Thompson_1994,_pg._301-178) Stewart's winning the Oscar "was considered a gold-plated apology for his being robbed of the award" for the previous year's *[Mr. Smith Goes to Washington](/wiki/Mr._Smith_Goes_to_Washington "Mr. Smith Goes to Washington")*.[[159]](#cite_note-auto-179)[[160]](#cite_note-180) Grant's not being nominated for *His Girl Friday* the same year is also a "sin of omission" for the Oscars.[[159]](#cite_note-auto-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg)

With [Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine") in *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941)

The following year Grant was considered for the [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for *[Penny Serenade](/wiki/Penny_Serenade "Penny Serenade")*—his first nomination from the academy. Wansell claims that Grant found the film to be an emotional experience, because he and wife-to-be [Barbara Hutton](/wiki/Barbara_Hutton "Barbara Hutton") had started to discuss having their own children.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell2011120-181) Later that year he appeared in the romantic psychological thriller *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")*, the first of Grant's four collaborations with director [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"). Grant did not warm to co-star [Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine"), finding her to be temperamental and unprofessional.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell2011122-182) Film critic [Bosley Crowther](/wiki/Bosley_Crowther "Bosley Crowther") of *The New York Times* considered that Grant was "provokingly irresponsible, boyishly gay and also oddly mysterious, as the role properly demands".[[163]](#cite_note-183) Hitchcock later stated that he thought the conventional happy ending of the film (with the wife discovering her husband is innocent rather than his being guilty and she letting him kill her with a glass of poisoned milk) "a complete mistake because of making that story with Cary Grant. Unless you have a cynical ending it makes the story too simple".[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2008124-184) Geoff Andrew of *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* believes *Suspicion* served as "a supreme example of Grant's ability to be simultaneously charming and sinister".[[165]](#cite_note-185)

In 1942, Grant participated in a three-week tour of the United States as part of a group to help the war effort and was photographed visiting wounded marines in hospital. He appeared in several routines of his own during these shows and often played the [straight man](/wiki/Straight_man "Straight man") opposite [Bert Lahr](/wiki/Bert_Lahr "Bert Lahr").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner197312,_18-186) In May 1942, when he was 38, the ten-minute propaganda short *[Road to Victory](/wiki/Road_to_Victory_(1944_film) "Road to Victory (1944 film)")* was released, in which he appeared alongside [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") and [Charles Ruggles](/wiki/Charles_Ruggles "Charles Ruggles").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973270-187) On film, Grant played Leopold Dilg, a convict on the run in *[The Talk of the Town](/wiki/The_Talk_of_the_Town_(1942_film) "The Talk of the Town (1942 film)")* (1942), who escapes after being wrongly convicted of arson and murder. He hides in a house with characters played by Jean Arthur and [Ronald Colman](/wiki/Ronald_Colman "Ronald Colman"), and gradually plots to secure his freedom. Crowther praised the script, and noted that Grant played Dilg with a "casualness which is slightly disturbing".[[168]](#cite_note-188) After a role as a foreign correspondent opposite [Ginger Rogers](/wiki/Ginger_Rogers "Ginger Rogers") and [Walter Slezak](/wiki/Walter_Slezak "Walter Slezak") in the off-beat comedy *[Once Upon a Honeymoon](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Honeymoon "Once Upon a Honeymoon")*,[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell2011132-189) in which he was praised for his scenes with Rogers,[[170]](#cite_note-190) he appeared in *[Mr. Lucky](/wiki/Mr._Lucky_(film) "Mr. Lucky (film)")* the following year, playing a gambler in a casino aboard a ship.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel199882–84-191) The commercially successful submarine war film *[Destination Tokyo](/wiki/Destination_Tokyo "Destination Tokyo")* (1943) was shot in just six weeks in September and October, which left him exhausted;[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner197312Wansell2011138-192) the reviewer from *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* thought it was one of the finest performances of his career.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973169-193)

In 1944, Grant starred alongside [Priscilla Lane](/wiki/Priscilla_Lane "Priscilla Lane"), [Raymond Massey](/wiki/Raymond_Massey "Raymond Massey") and [Peter Lorre](/wiki/Peter_Lorre "Peter Lorre"),[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBubbeo2001140-194) in [Frank Capra](/wiki/Frank_Capra "Frank Capra")'s dark comedy *[Arsenic and Old Lace](/wiki/Arsenic_and_Old_Lace_(film) "Arsenic and Old Lace (film)")*, playing the manic Mortimer Brewster, who belongs to a bizarre family that includes two murderous aunts and an uncle claiming to be President Teddy Roosevelt.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards2014242-195) Grant took up the role after it was originally offered to [Bob Hope](/wiki/Bob_Hope "Bob Hope"), who turned it down owing to schedule conflicts.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMell200521-196) Grant found the macabre subject matter of the film difficult to contend with and believed that it was the worst performance of his career.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997175–176-197) That year he received his second Oscar nomination for a role, opposite [Ethel Barrymore](/wiki/Ethel_Barrymore "Ethel Barrymore") and [Barry Fitzgerald](/wiki/Barry_Fitzgerald "Barry Fitzgerald") in the [Clifford Odets](/wiki/Clifford_Odets "Clifford Odets")-directed film *[None but the Lonely Heart](/wiki/None_but_the_Lonely_Heart_(film) "None but the Lonely Heart (film)")*, set in London during the Depression.[[178]](#cite_note-CG44-198) Late in the year he featured in the CBS Radio series *[Suspense](/wiki/Suspense_(radio_drama)#1942–1962 "Suspense (radio drama)")*, playing a tormented character who hysterically discovers that his amnesia has affected the masculine order in society in *[The Black Curtain](/wiki/The_Black_Curtain "The Black Curtain")*.[[179]](#cite_note-199)

### 1946–1953: Post-War success and slump

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1946–1953: Post-War success and slump")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Cary_Grant_%26_Ingrid_Bergman_Notorious.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_%26_Ingrid_Bergman_Notorious.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_%26_Ingrid_Bergman_Notorious.jpg)

Grant and [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman") in *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946)

After making a brief cameo appearance opposite [Claudette Colbert](/wiki/Claudette_Colbert "Claudette Colbert") in *[Without Reservations](/wiki/Without_Reservations "Without Reservations")* (1946),[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalliwellWalker2001520-200) Grant portrayed [Cole Porter](/wiki/Cole_Porter "Cole Porter") in the musical *[Night and Day](/wiki/Night_and_Day_(1946_film) "Night and Day (1946 film)")* (1946).[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001162-201) The production proved to be problematic, with scenes often requiring multiple takes, frustrating the cast and crew.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001162-201) Grant next appeared with [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman") and [Claude Rains](/wiki/Claude_Rains "Claude Rains") in the Hitchcock-directed film *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946), playing a government agent who recruits the American daughter of a convicted Nazi spy (Bergman) to infiltrate a Nazi organization in Brazil after World War II.[[182]](#cite_note-202) During the course of the film Grant and Bergman's characters fall in love and share one of the longest kisses in film history at around two and a half minutes.[[183]](#cite_note-203)[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly2014215-204) Wansell notes how Grant's performance "underlined how far his unique qualities as a screen actor had matured in the years since *The Awful Truth*".[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell199699-205)

In 1947, Grant played an artist who becomes involved in a court case when charged with assault in the comedy *[The Bachelor and the Bobby-Soxer](/wiki/The_Bachelor_and_the_Bobby-Soxer "The Bachelor and the Bobby-Soxer")* (released in the U.K. as "Bachelor Knight"), opposite [Myrna Loy](/wiki/Myrna_Loy "Myrna Loy") and [Shirley Temple](/wiki/Shirley_Temple "Shirley Temple").[[186]](#cite_note-206)[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeider2011265-207) The film was praised by the critics, who admired the picture's [slapstick](/wiki/Slapstick "Slapstick") qualities and chemistry between Grant and Loy;[[188]](#cite_note-208) it became one of the biggest-selling films at the box office that year.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997195-209) Later that year he starred opposite [David Niven](/wiki/David_Niven "David Niven") and [Loretta Young](/wiki/Loretta_Young "Loretta Young") in the comedy *[The Bishop's Wife](/wiki/The_Bishop%27s_Wife "The Bishop's Wife")*, playing an angel who is sent down from heaven to straighten out the relationship between the bishop (Niven) and his wife (Loretta Young).[[190]](#cite_note-Life48-210) The film was a major commercial and critical success, and was nominated for five Academy Awards.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997194-211) *Life* magazine called it "intelligently written and competently acted".[[190]](#cite_note-Life48-210)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Cary_Grant_Myrna_Loy_Mr_Blandings_Builds_His_Dream_House_1948.JPG/250px-Cary_Grant_Myrna_Loy_Mr_Blandings_Builds_His_Dream_House_1948.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Myrna_Loy_Mr_Blandings_Builds_His_Dream_House_1948.JPG)

Grant and [Myrna Loy](/wiki/Myrna_Loy "Myrna Loy") publicity photo for *[Mr. Blandings Builds His Dream House](/wiki/Mr._Blandings_Builds_His_Dream_House "Mr. Blandings Builds His Dream House")* (1948)

The following year, Grant played neurotic Jim Blandings, the title-sake in the comedy *[Mr. Blandings Builds His Dream House](/wiki/Mr._Blandings_Builds_His_Dream_House "Mr. Blandings Builds His Dream House")*, again with Loy. Though the film lost money for RKO,[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeider2011226-212) [Philip T. Hartung](/wiki/Philip_T._Hartung "Philip T. Hartung") of *Commonweal* thought that Grant's role as the "frustrated advertising man" was one of his best screen portrayals.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973196-213) In *[Every Girl Should Be Married](/wiki/Every_Girl_Should_Be_Married "Every Girl Should Be Married")*, an "airy comedy", he appeared with Betsy Drake and [Franchot Tone](/wiki/Franchot_Tone "Franchot Tone"), playing a bachelor who is trapped into marriage by Drake's conniving character.[[194]](#cite_note-Maltin1995-214) He finished the year as the fourth most popular film star at the box office.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997212-215) In 1949, Grant starred alongside [Ann Sheridan](/wiki/Ann_Sheridan "Ann Sheridan") in the comedy *[I Was a Male War Bride](/wiki/I_Was_a_Male_War_Bride "I Was a Male War Bride")* in which he appeared in scenes dressed as a woman, wearing a skirt and a wig.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBenshoffGriffin2011348-216) During the filming he was taken ill with infectious [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis") and lost weight, affecting the way he looked in the picture.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErickson2012274-217) The film, based on the autobiography of Belgian [resistance fighter](/wiki/Belgian_Resistance "Belgian Resistance") [Roger Charlier](/wiki/Roger_Charlier "Roger Charlier"), proved to be successful, becoming the highest-grossing film for 20th Century Fox that year with over $4.5 million in takings and being likened to Hawks's screwball comedies of the late 1930s.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997195-209) By this point he was one of the highest paid Hollywood stars, commanding $300,000 per picture.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell2011163-218)

The early 1950s marked the beginning of a slump in Grant's career.[[199]](#cite_note-TCMDW-219)[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001192-220) His roles as a top brain surgeon who is caught in the middle of a bitter revolution in a Latin American country in *[Crisis](/wiki/Crisis_(1950_film) "Crisis (1950 film)")*,[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHansonDunkleberger1999509-221) and as a medical-school professor and orchestra conductor opposite [Jeanne Crain](/wiki/Jeanne_Crain "Jeanne Crain") in *[People Will Talk](/wiki/People_Will_Talk "People Will Talk")* were poorly received.[[202]](#cite_note-222)[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973207–209-223) Grant had become tired of being Cary Grant after twenty years, being successful, wealthy and popular, and remarked: "To play yourself, your *true* self, is the hardest thing in the world".[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997197-224) In 1952, Grant starred in the comedy *[Room for One More](/wiki/Room_for_One_More_(film) "Room for One More (film)")*, playing an engineer husband who, with his wife ([Betsy Drake](/wiki/Betsy_Drake "Betsy Drake")), adopt two children from an orphanage.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996116-225)[[206]](#cite_note-226) He reunited with Howard Hawks to film the off-beat comedy *[Monkey Business](/wiki/Monkey_Business_(1952_film) "Monkey Business (1952 film)")*, co-starring Ginger Rogers and [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe").[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShevey1990204-227) Though the critic from *[Motion Picture Herald](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Herald "Motion Picture Herald")* wrote gushingly that Grant had given a career's best with an "extraordinary and agile performance", which was matched by Rogers,[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973214-228) it received a mixed reception overall.[[u]](#cite_note-230) Grant had hoped that starring opposite [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr") in the romantic comedy *[Dream Wife](/wiki/Dream_Wife "Dream Wife")* would salvage his career,[[199]](#cite_note-TCMDW-219) but it was a critical and financial failure upon release in July 1953, when Grant was 49. Though he was offered the leading part in *[A Star is Born](/wiki/A_Star_is_Born_(1954_film) "A Star is Born (1954 film)")*, Grant decided against playing that character. He believed that his film career was over, and briefly left the industry.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997211–212-231)

### 1955–1966: Film resurgence and final roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1955–1966: Film resurgence and final roles")]

In 1955, Grant agreed to star opposite [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly") in *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")*, playing a retired jewel thief named John Robie, nicknamed "The Cat", living in the [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera").[[211]](#cite_note-232) Grant and Kelly worked well together during the production, which was one of the most enjoyable experiences of Grant's career. He found Hitchcock and Kelly to be very professional,[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997214-233) and later stated that Kelly was "possibly the finest actress I've ever worked with".[[213]](#cite_note-86interview-234)[[v]](#cite_note-235) Grant was one of the first actors to go independent by not renewing his studio contract,[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProno2008127-236) effectively leaving the [studio system](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system"), which almost completely controlled all aspects of an actor's life.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHollinger201342-237) He decided which films he was going to appear in, often had personal choice of directors and co-stars, and at times negotiated a share of the gross revenue, something uncommon at the time.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997218-238) Grant received more than $700,000 for his 10% of the gross of the successful *To Catch a Thief*, while Hitchcock received less than $50,000 for directing and producing it.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHodgins1957146-239) Though critical reception to the overall film was mixed, Grant received high praise for his performance, with critics commenting on his suave, handsome appearance in the film.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997218-238)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Cary_Grant%2C_actor_USA%2C_at_Kastrup_Airport.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant%2C_actor_USA%2C_at_Kastrup_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant,_actor_USA,_at_Kastrup_Airport.jpg)

Grant in 1956

In 1957, Grant starred opposite [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr") in the romance *[An Affair to Remember](/wiki/An_Affair_to_Remember "An Affair to Remember")*, playing an international playboy who becomes the object of her affections. Schickel sees the film as one of the definitive romantic pictures of the period, but remarks that Grant was not entirely successful in trying to supersede the film's "gushing sentimentality".[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel1998112-240) That year, Grant also appeared opposite [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren") in *[The Pride and the Passion](/wiki/The_Pride_and_the_Passion "The Pride and the Passion")*. He had expressed an interest in playing [William Holden](/wiki/William_Holden "William Holden")'s character in *[The Bridge on the River Kwai](/wiki/The_Bridge_on_the_River_Kwai "The Bridge on the River Kwai")* at the time, but found that it was not possible because of his commitment to *The Pride and the Passion*.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel1998109-241) The film was shot on location in Spain and was problematic, with co-star [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") irritating his colleagues and leaving the production after just a few weeks.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeigh2015236-242) Although Grant had an affair with Loren during filming, Grant's attempts to woo Loren to marry him during the production proved fruitless,[[w]](#cite_note-244) which led to him expressing anger when Paramount cast her opposite him in *[Houseboat](/wiki/Houseboat_(film) "Houseboat (film)")* (1958) as part of her contract.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990254-245) The sexual tension between the two was so great during the making of *Houseboat* that the producers found it almost impossible to make.[[221]](#cite_note-Thorpe-243) Later in 1958, Grant starred opposite Bergman in the romantic comedy *[Indiscreet](/wiki/Indiscreet_(1958_film) "Indiscreet (1958 film)")*, playing a successful financier who has an affair with a famous actress (Bergman) while pretending to be a married man.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2007214-246) During the filming he formed a closer friendship and gained new respect for her as an actress.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990266-247) Schickel stated that he thought the film was possibly the finest romantic comedy film of the era, and that Grant himself had professed that it was one of his personal favorites.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel1998115-248) Grant received his first of five [Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy") nominations for his performance and finished the year as the most popular film star at the box office.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997212-215)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/North_by_Northwest_Cary_Grant_airplane_chase.jpg/250px-North_by_Northwest_Cary_Grant_airplane_chase.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_by_Northwest_Cary_Grant_airplane_chase.jpg)

Grant chased by the crop duster during *North by Northwest* (1959)

In 1959, Grant starred in the Hitchcock-directed film *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")*, playing an advertising executive who becomes embroiled in a case of mistaken identity. Like *Indiscreet*,[[226]](#cite_note-249)[[227]](#cite_note-250) it was warmly received by the critics and was a major commercial success,[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990277MorecambeSterling2001242-251) and is now often listed as one of the greatest films of all time.[[x]](#cite_note-255) [Weiler](/wiki/A._H._Weiler "A. H. Weiler"), writing in *The New York Times*, praised Grant's performance, remarking that the actor "was never more at home than in this role of the advertising-man-on-the-lam" and handled the role "with professional aplomb and grace".[[232]](#cite_note-256) Grant wore one of his most well-known suits in the film, which became very popular, a fourteen-gauge, mid-gray, subtly plaid, worsted wool that was custom-made on [Savile Row](/wiki/Savile_Row "Savile Row").[[233]](#cite_note-257)[[234]](#cite_note-258) Grant finished the year playing a U.S. Navy submarine skipper opposite [Tony Curtis](/wiki/Tony_Curtis "Tony Curtis") in the comedy *[Operation Petticoat](/wiki/Operation_Petticoat "Operation Petticoat")*.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErickson2012202-259) The reviewer from *Daily Variety* saw Grant's comic portrayal as a classic example of how to attract the laughter of the audience without lines, remarking that "In this film, most of the gags play off him. It is his reaction, blank, startled, etc., always underplayed, that creates or releases the humor".[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973247-260) The film was major box office success, and in 1973, Deschner ranked the film as the highest earning film of Grant's career at the US box office, with takings of $9.5 million.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973274-261)

In 1960, Grant appeared opposite [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr"), [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum"), and [Jean Simmons](/wiki/Jean_Simmons "Jean Simmons") in *[The Grass Is Greener](/wiki/The_Grass_Is_Greener "The Grass Is Greener")*, which was shot in England at [Osterley Park](/wiki/Osterley_Park "Osterley Park") and [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios").[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilverman1996279-262) McCann notes that Grant took great relish in "mocking his aristocratic character's over-refined tastes and mannerisms",[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997223-263) though the film was panned and was seen as his worst since *Dream Wife*.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001247-264) In 1962, Grant starred in the romantic comedy *[That Touch of Mink](/wiki/That_Touch_of_Mink "That Touch of Mink")*, playing suave, wealthy businessman Philip Shayne romantically involved with an office worker, played by [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day"). He invites her to his apartment in [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"), but her guilty conscience begins to take hold.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrindon201135-265) The picture was praised by critics, and it received three Academy Award nominations and won the [Golden Globe Award for Best Comedy Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy"),[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMessina201262-266) in addition to landing Grant another Golden Globe Award nomination for Best Actor.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGee2005155-267) Deschner ranked the film as the second highest grossing of Grant's career.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973274-261)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Audrey_Hepburn_and_Cary_Grant_1.jpg/250px-Audrey_Hepburn_and_Cary_Grant_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Audrey_Hepburn_and_Cary_Grant_1.jpg)

Grant and [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn") in *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")* (1963)

Producers [Albert R. Broccoli](/wiki/Albert_R._Broccoli "Albert R. Broccoli") and [Harry Saltzman](/wiki/Harry_Saltzman "Harry Saltzman") originally sought Grant for the role of [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_(literary_character) "James Bond (literary character)") in *[Dr. No](/wiki/Dr._No_(film) "Dr. No (film)")* (1962) but discarded the idea as Grant would be committed to only one feature film; therefore, the producers decided to go after someone who could be part of a franchise after [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason") would only agree to commit to three films.[[244]](#cite_note-268) In 1963, Grant appeared in his last typically suave, romantic role opposite [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn") in *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")*.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonaco1992121-269) Grant found the experience of working with Hepburn "wonderful" and believed that their close relationship was clear on camera,[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997228-270) though according to Hepburn, he was particularly worried during the filming that he would be criticized for being far too old for her and seen as a "cradle snatcher".[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001254-271) Author Chris Barsanti writes: "It's the film's canny flirtatiousness that makes it such ingenious entertainment. Grant and Hepburn play off each other like the pros that they are".[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarsanti2010124-272) The film, well received by the critics,[[249]](#cite_note-273) is often called "the best Hitchcock film Hitchcock never made".[[250]](#cite_note-EmpireIanNathan-274)[[251]](#cite_note-275)[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEsquith2007210-276)

In 1964, Grant changed from his typically suave, distinguished screen persona to play a grizzled beachcomber who is coerced into serving as a [coastwatcher](/wiki/Coastwatcher "Coastwatcher") on an uninhabited island in the World War II romantic comedy *[Father Goose](/wiki/Father_Goose_(film) "Father Goose (film)")*.[[253]](#cite_note-277) The film was a major commercial success, and upon its release at Radio City at Christmas 1964 it took over $210,000 at the box-office in the first week, breaking the record set by *Charade* the previous year.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001263-278) Grant's final film, *[Walk, Don't Run](/wiki/Walk,_Don%27t_Run_(film) "Walk, Don't Run (film)")* (1966), a comedy co-starring [Jim Hutton](/wiki/Jim_Hutton "Jim Hutton") and [Samantha Eggar](/wiki/Samantha_Eggar "Samantha Eggar"), was shot on location in Tokyo,[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye1973139-279) and is set amid the backdrop of the housing shortage of the [1964 Tokyo Olympics](/wiki/1964_Tokyo_Olympics "1964 Tokyo Olympics").[[256]](#cite_note-Inc1966-280) *Newsweek* concluded: "Though Grant's personal presence is indispensable, the character he plays is almost wholly superfluous. Perhaps the inference to be taken is that a man in his 50s or 60s has no place in romantic comedy except as a catalyst. If so, the chemistry is wrong for everyone".[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973268-281) Hitchcock had asked Grant to star in *[Torn Curtain](/wiki/Torn_Curtain "Torn Curtain")* that year, only to learn that he had decided to retire.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoffin2014175-282)

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Later years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Cary_Grant_2_Allan_Warren.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_2_Allan_Warren.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_2_Allan_Warren.jpg)

Grant at 69 in 1973

In 1966, when his daughter [Jennifer Grant](/wiki/Jennifer_Grant "Jennifer Grant") was born, Grant retired from the screen so he could focus on bringing her up and to provide a sense of permanence and stability in her life.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996255-283) He had become increasingly disillusioned with cinema in the 1960s, rarely finding a script of which he approved. He remarked: "I could have gone on acting and playing a grandfather or a bum, but I discovered more important things in life".[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997233-284) He knew after he had made *Charade* that the "Golden Age" of Hollywood was over.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001259-285) Grant expressed little interest in making a career comeback, and would respond to the suggestion with "fat chance".[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997265Moore2009148-286) He did, however, briefly appear in the audience for [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")'s 1970 Las Vegas concert documentary *[Elvis: That's the Way It Is](/wiki/Elvis:_That%27s_the_Way_It_Is "Elvis: That's the Way It Is")*.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001295-287) In the 1970s, he was given the negatives from a number of his films, and he sold them to television for a sum of over $2 million in 1975 ($12 million in 2025).[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001294-288)

Morecambe and Stirling argue that Grant's absence from film after 1966 was not because he had "irrevocably turned his back on the film industry," but because he was "caught between a decision made and the temptation to eat a bit of humble pie and re-announce himself to the cinema-going public".[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001295–296-289) In the 1970s, MGM was keen on remaking *[Grand Hotel](/wiki/Grand_Hotel_(1932_film) "Grand Hotel (1932 film)")* (1932) and hoped to lure Grant out of retirement. Hitchcock had long wanted to make a film based on the idea of *Hamlet*, with Grant in the lead role.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001296-290) Grant stated that [Warren Beatty](/wiki/Warren_Beatty "Warren Beatty") had made a big effort to get him to play the role of Mr. Jordan in *[Heaven Can Wait](/wiki/Heaven_Can_Wait_(1978_film) "Heaven Can Wait (1978 film)")* (1978), which eventually went to James Mason.[[213]](#cite_note-86interview-234) Morecambe and Stirling claim that Grant had also expressed an interest in appearing in *[A Touch of Class](/wiki/A_Touch_of_Class_(film) "A Touch of Class (film)")* (1973), *[The Verdict](/wiki/The_Verdict "The Verdict")* (1982), and a film adaptation of [William Goldman](/wiki/William_Goldman "William Goldman")'s 1983 book about screenwriting, *[Adventures in the Screen Trade](/wiki/Adventures_in_the_Screen_Trade "Adventures in the Screen Trade")*.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001295–296-289)

In the late 1970s and early '80s, Grant became troubled by the deaths of many close friends, including [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes") in 1976, [Howard Hawks](/wiki/Howard_Hawks "Howard Hawks") in 1977, [Lord Mountbatten](/wiki/Lord_Mountbatten "Lord Mountbatten") and Barbara Hutton in 1979, Alfred Hitchcock in 1980, Grace Kelly and Ingrid Bergman in 1982, and [David Niven](/wiki/David_Niven "David Niven") in 1983. At the [funeral of Mountbatten](/wiki/Funeral_of_Louis_Mountbatten,_1st_Earl_Mountbatten_of_Burma "Funeral of Louis Mountbatten, 1st Earl Mountbatten of Burma"), he was quoted as remarking to a friend: "I'm absolutely pooped, and I'm so goddamned old. ...I'm going to quit all next year. I'm going to lie in bed... I shall just close all doors, turn off the telephone, and enjoy my life".[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997264-291) Grace Kelly's death was the hardest on him, as it was unexpected and the two had remained close friends after filming *To Catch a Thief*.[[y]](#cite_note-293) Grant visited Monaco three or four times each year during his retirement,[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001319Grant201152-294) and showed his support for Kelly by joining the board of the [Princess Grace Foundation](/wiki/Princess_Grace_Foundation "Princess Grace Foundation").[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001319-292)

In 1980, the [Los Angeles County Museum of Art](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Museum_of_Art "Los Angeles County Museum of Art") put on a two-month retrospective of more than 40 of Grant's films.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997265-295) In 1982, he was honored with the "Man of the Year" award by the [New York Friars Club](/wiki/New_York_Friars_Club "New York Friars Club") at the [Waldorf-Astoria Hotel](/wiki/Waldorf-Astoria_Hotel "Waldorf-Astoria Hotel").[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001299-296) He turned 80 on 18 January 1984, and [Peter Bogdanovich](/wiki/Peter_Bogdanovich "Peter Bogdanovich") noticed that a "serenity" had come over him.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997273-297) Grant was in good health until he had a mild stroke in October that year.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonnelley2003292-298) In the last few years of his life, he undertook tours of the United States in the one-man show *A Conversation with Cary Grant*, in which he would show clips from his films and answer audience questions.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrachtenbergJaynes2004-299)[[275]](#cite_note-Decker,_Cathleen-300) He made some 36 public appearances in his last four years, from New Jersey to Texas, and his audiences ranged from elderly film buffs to enthusiastic college students discovering his films for the first time. Grant admitted that the appearances were "ego-fodder", remarking that "I know who I am inside and outside, but it's nice to have the outside, at least, substantiated".[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997270-301)

## Critical reception and commentary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Critical reception and commentary")]

Film critic [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael"), a fan of Grant's, once described him as "the most publicly seduced male the world has ever known." Of his persona, Kael wrote: "The little bit of shyness and reserve in Grant is pure box-office gold, and being the pursued doesn't make him seem weak or passively soft.
