It makes him glamorous, and since he's not available as other men, far more desirable."[[277]](#cite_note-302)

## Business interests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Business interests")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg/250px-North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg)

[James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason"), [Eva Marie Saint](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint") and Grant at [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") during *North by Northwest* (1959)

Stirling refers to Grant as "one of the shrewdest businessmen ever to operate in Hollywood".[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001xviii-303) His long-term friendship with Howard Hughes from the 1930s onward saw him invited into the most glamorous circles in Hollywood and their lavish parties.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199784-304) Biographers Morecambe and Stirling state that Hughes played a major role in the development of Grant's business interests so that by 1939, he was "already an astute operator with various commercial interests".[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling200197–98-305) Scott also played a role, encouraging Grant to invest his money in shares, making him a wealthy man by the end of the 1930s.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201153-158) In the 1940s, Grant and Barbara Hutton invested heavily in real estate development in [Acapulco](/wiki/Acapulco "Acapulco") at a time when it was little more than a fishing village,[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990152-306) and teamed up with [Richard Widmark](/wiki/Richard_Widmark "Richard Widmark"), [Roy Rogers](/wiki/Roy_Rogers "Roy Rogers"), and [Red Skelton](/wiki/Red_Skelton "Red Skelton") to buy a hotel there.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterFoster200096-307) Behind his business interests was a particularly intelligent mind, to the point that his friend David Niven once said: "Before computers went into general release, Cary had one in his brain".[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling200197–98-305) Film critic [David Thomson](/wiki/David_Thomson_(film_critic) "David Thomson (film critic)") believes that Grant's intelligence came across on screen, and stated that "no one else looked so good and so intelligent at the same time".[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001200-308)

After Grant retired from the screen, he became more active in business. He accepted a position on the board of directors at [Fabergé](/wiki/Faberg%C3%A9_(cosmetics) "Fabergé (cosmetics)").[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBernsteinHammRubini2011211-309) This position was not honorary, as some had assumed; Grant regularly attended meetings and traveled internationally to support them.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996277Guttman201513-310) His pay was modest in comparison to the millions of his film career, a salary of a reported $15,000 a year.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001289-311) Such was Grant's influence on the company that [George Barrie](/wiki/George_Barrie "George Barrie") once claimed that Grant had played a role in the growth of the firm to annual revenues of about $50 million in 1968, a growth of nearly 80% since the inaugural year in 1964.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997243-312) The position also permitted the use of a private plane, which Grant could use to fly to see his daughter wherever her mother, Dyan Cannon, was working.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2014103-313)

In 1975, Grant was an appointed director of MGM. In 1980, he sat on the board of MGM Films and MGM Grand Hotels following the division of the parent company. He played an active role in the promotion of [MGM Grand Hotel](/wiki/MGM_Grand_Las_Vegas "MGM Grand Las Vegas") in Las Vegas when it opened in 1973, and he continued to promote the city throughout the 1970s.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001290-314) When [Allan Warren](/wiki/Allan_Warren "Allan Warren") met Grant for a photo shoot that year he noticed how tired Grant looked, and his "slightly melancholic air".[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001292–293-315) Grant later joined the boards of [Hollywood Park](/wiki/Pinnacle_Entertainment "Pinnacle Entertainment"), the Academy of Magical Arts ([The Magic Castle](/wiki/The_Magic_Castle "The Magic Castle"), Hollywood, California), and Western Airlines (acquired by [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines") in 1987).[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrachtenbergJaynes2004-299)[[291]](#cite_note-Fristoe-316)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Cary_Grant_and_Margaux_Hemingway%2C_1976.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_and_Margaux_Hemingway%2C_1976.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_and_Margaux_Hemingway,_1976.jpg)

With friend [Margaux Hemingway](/wiki/Margaux_Hemingway "Margaux Hemingway") in 1976

Grant became a [naturalized United States citizen](/wiki/Naturalized_United_States_citizen "Naturalized United States citizen") on 26 June 1942, aged 38, at which time he also legally changed his name to Cary Grant.[[292]](#cite_note-317)[[293]](#cite_note-318) At the time of his naturalization, he listed his middle name as Alexander rather than Alec.[[3]](#cite_note-McCarthy-3)

One of the wealthiest stars in Hollywood, Grant owned houses in [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills "Beverly Hills"), [Malibu](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), and [Palm Springs](/wiki/Palm_Springs "Palm Springs").[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGovoni1973207-319) He was immaculate in his personal grooming, and [Edith Head](/wiki/Edith_Head "Edith Head"), the renowned Hollywood costume designer, appreciated his "meticulous" attention to detail and considered him to have had the greatest fashion sense of any actor she had worked with.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997178–179-320) McCann attributed his "almost obsessive maintenance" with tanning, which deepened the older he got,[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling200123-321) to Douglas Fairbanks, who also had a major influence on his refined sense of dress.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199767-322) McCann notes that because Grant came from a working-class background and was not well educated, he made a particular effort over the course of his career to mix with high society and absorb their knowledge, manners, and etiquette.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199764–65-323) His image was meticulously crafted from the early days in Hollywood, where he frequently sunbathed; he avoided being photographed smoking despite smoking two packs a day at the time.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201132-324) Grant quit smoking in the early 1950s through [hypnotherapy](/wiki/Hypnotherapy "Hypnotherapy").[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996122-325) He remained health-conscious, staying very trim and athletic even into his late career, though he claimed that he "never crook[ed] a finger to keep fit",[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001143-326) saying that he did "everything in moderation. Except making love."[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001317-327)

Grant's daughter, Jennifer, said that her father made hundreds of friends from all walks of life, and that their house was frequently visited by the likes of Frank and [Barbara Sinatra](/wiki/Barbara_Sinatra "Barbara Sinatra"), [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones"), [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") and his wife [Veronique](/wiki/Veronique_Peck "Veronique Peck"), [Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson") and his wife, [Kirk Kerkorian](/wiki/Kirk_Kerkorian "Kirk Kerkorian"), and [Merv Griffin](/wiki/Merv_Griffin "Merv Griffin"). She said that Grant and Sinatra were the closest of friends, that the two men had a similar radiance and "indefinable incandescence of charm", and that they were eternally "high on life".[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant201143-328) Grant became a fan of comedians [Morecambe and Wise](/wiki/Morecambe_and_Wise "Morecambe and Wise") in the 1960s, and remained friends with [Eric Morecambe](/wiki/Eric_Morecambe "Eric Morecambe") until his death in 1984.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001260-329)

While raising Jennifer, Grant archived artifacts of her childhood and adolescence in a bank-quality, room-sized vault he had installed in the house. Jennifer attributed this meticulous collection to the fact that artifacts of his own childhood had been destroyed during the [Luftwaffe's bombing of Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_Blitz "Bristol Blitz") in World War II (an event that also claimed the lives of his uncle, aunt, cousin, and his cousin's husband and grandson), and he may have wanted to prevent her from experiencing a similar loss.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant2011234,_263-330)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/3c/Randolph_Scott_and_Cary_Grant_over_a_seafood_lunch.jpg/250px-Randolph_Scott_and_Cary_Grant_over_a_seafood_lunch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Randolph_Scott_and_Cary_Grant_over_a_seafood_lunch.jpg)

Randolph Scott *(left)* and Grant in 1933 (from *[Modern Screen](/wiki/Modern_Screen "Modern Screen")* promotional feature)

Grant lived with costume designer [Orry-Kelly](/wiki/Orry-Kelly "Orry-Kelly") from 1925 to 1931 in the West Village, New York, until both moved to Hollywood. They met when Grant was a struggling performer who had just been evicted from a boarding house for nonpayment; they had a volatile, on-and-off relationship over three decades until Orry-Kelly died in 1964, when Grant became one of his pallbearers.[[306]](#cite_note-331)[[307]](#cite_note-332) While Kelly stops short of claiming that Grant was his boyfriend in his memoir, director Gillian Armstrong's documentary on Kelly's memoir states so outright.[[308]](#cite_note-333)

Grant lived with actor [Randolph Scott](/wiki/Randolph_Scott "Randolph Scott") off and on for 12 years.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley199057Schickel199844Laurents2001131Mann2001154Prono2008126Guilbert2009126-334) The two met early in Grant's career, in 1932, at the Paramount studio when Scott was filming *[Sky Bride](/wiki/Sky_Bride "Sky Bride")* while Grant was shooting *[Sinners in the Sun](/wiki/Sinners_in_the_Sun "Sinners in the Sun");* they moved in together soon afterwards.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBraun20071920-335) Whether the relationship was romantic is a matter of biographical dispute.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENott200412-336) [Richard Blackwell](/wiki/Richard_Blackwell "Richard Blackwell"), then an actor at RKO, [Jerome Zerbe](/wiki/Jerome_Zerbe "Jerome Zerbe"), a photographer who shot a series of publicity photographs of the men in their home, and [Scotty Bowers](/wiki/Scotty_Bowers "Scotty Bowers"), a Hollywood pimp, all claimed to have slept with the pair. Blackwell wrote in his autobiography that Grant and Scott "were deeply, madly in love, their devotion was complete".[[312]](#cite_note-337)[[313]](#cite_note-338)[[314]](#cite_note-339) Biographer and friend of Grant's, Bill Royce, claimed that in old age Grant confessed to him that he and Scott had been bisexual, and that their relationship was the first time he had ever been in love, characterising the memory as: "Have you ever heard of gravity collapse?" However, he allegedly also told Royce that while Scott had loved him "on some profound level", Scott had not desired him physically to the same degree, but that they had explored the imbalance of their attraction. Since Grant's death, journalists such as David Canfield writing for *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* have revisited the rumours and speculation.[[315]](#cite_note-340) Grant's daughter, Jennifer, has denied her father was bisexual.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant201187-341) His ex-wife [Dyan Cannon](/wiki/Dyan_Cannon "Dyan Cannon") believed he was queer.[[317]](#cite_note-auto1-342) When [Chevy Chase](/wiki/Chevy_Chase "Chevy Chase") joked on television in 1980 that Grant was a "homo. What a gal!", Grant sued him for slander, and Chase was forced to retract his words.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997307Seymour2009114–115-343)

Grant began experimenting with [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD") in the late 1950s,[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel19984-344) before it became more widely popular. His wife at the time, Betsy Drake, displayed a keen interest in psychotherapy, and through her Grant developed a considerable knowledge of the field of [psychoanalysis](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis"). Radiologist Mortimer Hartman began treating him with LSD in the late 1950s, with Grant optimistic that it could make him feel better about himself and rid him of the inner turmoil from his childhood and failed relationships. He had an estimated 100 sessions over several years.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997205–206-345) For a long time, Grant viewed the drug positively, saying that it was the solution after many years of "searching for his peace of mind", and that for the first time in his life he was "truly, deeply and honestly happy".[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997205–206-345) Dyan Cannon claimed during a court hearing that he was an "apostle of LSD", and that he was still taking the drug in 1967 as part of a remedy to save their relationship.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997239-346) Grant later remarked that "taking LSD was an utterly foolish thing to do but I was a self-opinionated boor, hiding all kinds of layers and defences, hypocrisy and vanity. I had to get rid of them and wipe the slate clean."[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001215-347)

### Marriages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Marriages")]

Grant was married five times.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrury200851-348) He wed [Virginia Cherrill](/wiki/Virginia_Cherrill "Virginia Cherrill") on 9 February 1934, at the [Caxton Hall](/wiki/Caxton_Hall "Caxton Hall") [register office](/wiki/Register_office "Register office") in London.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201135-349) She divorced him on 26 March 1935,[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHouseman1991128-350) following charges that he had hit her.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot2004249-351) They were involved in a bitter divorce case which was widely reported in the press, with Cherrill demanding $1,000 a week from him in benefits from his Paramount earnings.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201138-124) Grant then dated actress [Phyllis Brooks](/wiki/Phyllis_Brooks "Phyllis Brooks") from 1937. They considered marriage and vacationed together in Europe in mid-1939, visiting the Roman villa of Dorothy Taylor Dentice di Frasso in Italy, but the relationship ended later that year.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201157-352)

He married [Barbara Hutton](/wiki/Barbara_Hutton "Barbara Hutton") in 1942,[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeymour2009260-353) one of the wealthiest women in the world, following a $50 million inheritance from her grandfather [Frank Winfield Woolworth](/wiki/Frank_Winfield_Woolworth "Frank Winfield Woolworth").[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGressorCook2005259-354) They were derisively nicknamed "Cash and Cary",[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcIntoshWeaver198341-355) although Grant refused any financial settlement in a prenuptial agreement[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeymann1987294-356) to avoid the accusation that he married for money.[[z]](#cite_note-358) Toward the end of their marriage they lived in a white mansion at 10615 Bellagio Road in Bel Air.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990183Chase200497-359) They divorced in 1945, although they remained the "fondest of friends".[[334]](#cite_note-360) He dated Betty Hensel for a period,[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1983189-361) then married [Betsy Drake](/wiki/Betsy_Drake "Betsy Drake"), the co-star of two of his films, on 25 December 1949. In 1957 Grant had an affair with [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren").[[336]](#cite_note-362) Drake and Grant separated in 1958,[[337]](#cite_note-363) divorcing on 14 August 1962.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParish2010200-364) It was his longest marriage.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel200928-365)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Cary_Grant_Betsy_Drake_Dick_Stabile_1955.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_Betsy_Drake_Dick_Stabile_1955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Betsy_Drake_Dick_Stabile_1955.jpg)

Grant with [Betsy Drake](/wiki/Betsy_Drake "Betsy Drake") and saxophonist [Dick Stabile](/wiki/Dick_Stabile "Dick Stabile") *(right)* in 1955

Grant married [Dyan Cannon](/wiki/Dyan_Cannon "Dyan Cannon") on 22 July 1965, at the [Desert Inn](/wiki/Desert_Inn "Desert Inn") in Las Vegas,[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley1990312Drury200852-366) and their daughter [Jennifer](/wiki/Jennifer_Grant "Jennifer Grant"), his only child, was born on 26 February 1966;[[341]](#cite_note-ew-367) he frequently called her his "best production".[[342]](#cite_note-368) He said of fatherhood:

> My life changed the day Jennifer was born. I've come to think that the reason we're put on this earth is to procreate. To leave something behind. Not films, because you know that I don't think my films will last very long once I'm gone. But another human being. That's what's important.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997237-369)

Grant and Cannon separated in August 1967.[[344]](#cite_note-370) Grant was emotionally abusive towards Cannon.[[317]](#cite_note-auto1-342)

On 12 March 1968, Grant was involved in a car accident in Queens, New York, en route to [JFK Airport](/wiki/JFK_Airport "JFK Airport"), when a truck hit the side of his limousine. He was hospitalized for 17 days with three broken ribs and bruising. A female companion, Baroness Gratia von Furstenberg, was also injured in the accident.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcIntoshWeaver198365-371)[[346]](#cite_note-372) Grant and Cannon divorced nine days later.[[347]](#cite_note-373)

Grant had a brief affair with actress [Cynthia Bouron](/wiki/Cynthia_Bouron "Cynthia Bouron") in the late 1960s.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcIntoshWeaver198315Eliot200414–15-374) He had been at odds with the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") since 1958, but received an Academy Honorary Award in 1970.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200414–15-375) He announced that he would attend the awards ceremony to accept it, ending his 12-year boycott of the ceremony. Two days after his announcement, Bouron filed a paternity suit against him and publicly stated that he was the father of her seven-week-old daughter,[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200414–15-375)[[aa]](#cite_note-377) and she named him as the father on the child's birth certificate.[[351]](#cite_note-films-378) Grant challenged her to a blood test and Bouron failed to provide one, and the court ordered her to remove his name from the certificate.[[351]](#cite_note-films-378)[[352]](#cite_note-379)[[ab]](#cite_note-381) Between 1973 and 1977, he dated British photojournalist Maureen Donaldson,[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHofstede1994194-382) followed by the much younger Victoria Morgan.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyceDonaldson1989131-383)

On 11 April 1981, Grant married Barbara Harris, a British hotel public relations agent 46 years his junior.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996281Roberts2014106-384) They met in 1976 at the [Royal Lancaster Hotel](/wiki/Royal_Lancaster_Hotel "Royal Lancaster Hotel") in London, where Harris was working at the time and Grant was attending a Fabergé conference. They became friends, but it was not until 1979 that she moved to live with him in California. Grant's friends felt that she had a positive impact on him, and [Prince Rainier of Monaco](/wiki/Prince_Rainier_of_Monaco "Prince Rainier of Monaco") remarked that Grant had "never been happier" than he was in his last years with her.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001312–314-385)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Politics")]

Biographer Nancy Nelson noted that Grant did not openly align himself with political causes but occasionally commented on current events. Grant spoke out against the [blacklisting](/wiki/Blacklisting "Blacklisting") of his friend [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") during the period of [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism"), arguing that Chaplin was not a [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") and that his status as an entertainer was more important than his political beliefs. In 1950, he told a reporter that he would like to see a female president of the United States but asserted a reluctance to comment on political affairs, believing it was not the place of actors to do so.[[358]](#cite_note-386) Grant supported [Thomas Dewey](/wiki/Thomas_Dewey "Thomas Dewey") in the [1944 United States presidential election](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election"), appearing at a rally at the [Los Angeles Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Coliseum "Los Angeles Coliseum") after the New York governor won the Republican nomination.[[359]](#cite_note-387)

In 1963, Grant visited Washington, D.C., and joined Attorney General [Robert Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_Kennedy "Robert Kennedy") on a visit to a schoolyard and a nearby junkyard, as the two considered how transforming the space into a playground might benefit the city's children.[[360]](#cite_note-388)
On 10 December 1967, Grant attended the [Democratic National Committee](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee") Fundraising Dinner at the [Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Plaza_Hotel "Plaza Hotel") in New York City.

In 1976, Grant made a public appearance at the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") National Convention in [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") during which he gave a speech in support of [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford")'s reelection and for female equality before introducing [Betty Ford](/wiki/Betty_Ford "Betty Ford") onto the stage.[[361]](#cite_note-389)[[362]](#cite_note-390) A 1977 interview with Grant in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* noted his political beliefs to be conservative but observed that Grant did not actively campaign for candidates.[[363]](#cite_note-391)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg)

Still for *North by Northwest*

Grant was at the [Adler Theater](/wiki/Adler_Theater "Adler Theater") in [Davenport, Iowa](/wiki/Davenport,_Iowa "Davenport, Iowa"), on the afternoon of Saturday, 29 November 1986, preparing for his performance in *A Conversation with Cary Grant* when he was taken ill; he had been feeling unwell as he arrived at the theater. Basil Williams photographed him there and thought that he still looked his usual suave self, but he noticed that he seemed very tired and that he stumbled once in the auditorium. Williams recalls that Grant rehearsed for half an hour before "something seemed wrong" all of a sudden, and he disappeared backstage. Grant was taken back to the Blackhawk Hotel where he and his wife had checked in, and a doctor was called and discovered that Grant was having a massive stroke, with a blood pressure reading of 210 over 130. Grant refused to be taken to the hospital. The doctor recalled: "The stroke was getting worse. In only fifteen minutes he deteriorated rapidly. It was terrible watching him die and not being able to help. But he wouldn't let us." By 8:45 p.m., Grant had slipped into a coma and was taken to [St. Luke's Hospital](/wiki/St._Luke%27s_Hospital_(Davenport,_Iowa) "St. Luke's Hospital (Davenport, Iowa)") in Davenport, Iowa.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001323–324-392) He spent 45 minutes in the emergency room before being transferred to intensive care. He died at 11:22 pm, aged 82.[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001324-393)

> Death? Of course I think of it. But I don't want to dwell on it ... I think the thing you think about when you're my age is how you're going to do it and whether you'll behave well.

—Grant at age 73[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997274-394)

An editorial in *The New York Times* stated: "Cary Grant was not supposed to die. ... Cary Grant was supposed to stick around, our perpetual touchstone of charm and elegance and romance and youth."[[367]](#cite_note-nyt-editorial-395) His body was taken back to California, where it was cremated and his ashes scattered in the Pacific Ocean.[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001325-396) No funeral was conducted for him following his request, which Roderick Mann remarked was appropriate for "the private man who didn't want the nonsense of a funeral".[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997276-397) His estate was worth in the region of sixty to eighty million dollars;[[370]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell1996188McCann1997277-398) the bulk of it went to Barbara Harris and Jennifer.[[275]](#cite_note-Decker,_Cathleen-300)

## Screen persona

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Screen persona")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Cary_Grant_Indiscreet_1958.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_Indiscreet_1958.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Indiscreet_1958.jpg)

Grant in 1958

McCann wrote that one of the reasons why Grant's film career was so successful is that he was not conscious of how handsome he was on screen, acting in a fashion that was most unexpected and unusual from a Hollywood star of that period.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997104-399) George Cukor once stated: "You see, he didn't depend on his looks. He wasn't a narcissist, he acted as though he were just an ordinary young man. And that made it all the more appealing, that a handsome young man was funny; that was especially unexpected and good because we think, 'Well, if he's a [Beau Brummel](/wiki/Beau_Brummel "Beau Brummel"), he can't be either funny or intelligent', but he proved otherwise".[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997104-399) Jennifer Grant acknowledged that her father neither relied on his looks nor was a character actor, and said that he was just the opposite of that, playing the "basic man".[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant201167-400)

Grant's appeal was unusually broad among both men and women. [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") remarked that men wanted to be him and women dreamed of dating him. She noticed that Grant treated his female co-stars differently than many of the leading men at the time, regarding them as subjects with multiple qualities rather than "treating them as sex objects".[[102]](#cite_note-Kael-114) [Leslie Caron](/wiki/Leslie_Caron "Leslie Caron") said that he was the most talented leading man she worked with.[[373]](#cite_note-hattenstone20210621-401) [David Shipman](/wiki/David_Shipman_(writer) "David Shipman (writer)") writes that "more than most stars, he belonged to the public".[[374]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997284-402) A number of critics have argued that Grant had the rare star ability to turn a mediocre picture into a good one. Philip T. Hartung of *The Commonweal* stated in his review for *Mr. Lucky* (1943) that, if it "weren't for Cary Grant's persuasive personality, the whole thing would melt away to nothing at all".[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973166-403) Political theorist [C. L. R. James](/wiki/C._L._R._James "C. L. R. James") saw Grant as a "new and very important symbol", a new type of Englishman who differed from [Leslie Howard](/wiki/Leslie_Howard "Leslie Howard") and Ronald Colman, who represented the "freedom, natural grace, simplicity, and directness which characterise such different American types as Jimmy Stewart and Ronald Reagan", which ultimately symbolized the growing relationship between Britain and America.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997109-404)

> Once he realized that each movement could be stylized for humor, the eyepopping, the cocked head, the forward lunge, and the slightly ungainly stride became as certain as the pen strokes of a master cartoonist.

—Film critic [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") on the development of Grant's comic acting in the late 1930s[[102]](#cite_note-Kael-114)

McCann notes that Grant typically played "wealthy privileged characters who never seemed to have any need to work in order to maintain their glamorous and hedonistic lifestyle".[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997104-399) [Martin Stirling](/wiki/Martin_Stirling "Martin Stirling") thought that Grant had an acting range that was "greater than any of his contemporaries", but felt that a number of critics underrated him as an actor. He believes that Grant was always at his "physical and verbal best in situations that bordered on farce".[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001xvii,_174-405) Charles Champlin identifies a paradox in Grant's screen persona, in his unusual ability to "mix polish and pratfalls in successive scenes". He remarks that Grant was "refreshingly able to play the near-fool, the fey idiot, without compromising his masculinity or surrendering to camp for its own sake".[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner19733-406) Wansell further notes that Grant could, "with the arch of an eyebrow or the merest hint of a smile, question his own image".[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell20117-407) Stanley Donen stated that his real "magic" came from his attention to minute details and always seeming real, which came from "enormous amounts of work" rather than being God-given.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997128-408) Grant remarked of his career: "I guess to a certain extent I did eventually become the characters I was playing. I played at being someone I wanted to be until I became that person, or he became me".[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199759-409) He professed that the real Cary Grant was more like his scruffy, unshaven fisherman in *Father Goose* than the "well-tailored charmer" of *Charade*.[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997250-410)

Grant often poked fun at himself with statements such as, "Everyone wants to be Cary Grant—even I want to be Cary Grant",[[383]](#cite_note-411) and in ad-lib lines such as in *His Girl Friday* (1940): "Listen, the last man who said that to me was Archie Leach, just a week before he cut his throat."[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaklamanidouTally2014167-412) In *Arsenic and Old Lace* (1944), a gravestone is seen bearing the name Archie Leach.[[385]](#cite_note-413)[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalliwellWalker2001184-414) Alfred Hitchcock thought that Grant was very effective in darker roles, with a mysterious, dangerous quality, remarking that "there is a frightening side to Cary that no one can quite put their finger on".[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201110-415) Wansell notes that this darker, mysterious side extended to his personal life, which he took great lengths to cover up to retain his debonair image.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201110-415) In a profile, [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe") wrote that "Cary Grant plays a wonderful Cary Grant." Upon being recognized by a fan, Wolfe writes that Grant "cocks his head and gives her the Cary Grant mock-quizzical look—just like he does in the movies—the look that says, 'I don't know what's happening, but we're not going to take it very seriously, are we? Or are we?'"[[388]](#cite_note-416)

## Honors and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Honors and awards")]

> No other man seemed so classless and self-assured ... at ease with the romantic as the comic ... aged so well and with such fine style ... in short, played the part so well: Cary Grant made men seem like a good idea.

—Biographer Graham McCann on Cary Grant.[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann19973–4-417)

Biographers Morecambe and Stirling believe that Cary Grant was the "greatest leading man Hollywood had ever known".[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001287-418) Schickel stated that there are "very few stars who achieve the magnitude of Cary Grant, art of a very high and subtle order" and thought that he was the "best star actor there ever was in the movies".[[391]](#cite_note-419)[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchickel1998vi-420) [David Thomson](/wiki/David_Thomson_(film_critic) "David Thomson (film critic)") and directors [Stanley Donen](/wiki/Stanley_Donen "Stanley Donen") and [Howard Hawks](/wiki/Howard_Hawks "Howard Hawks") concurred that Grant was the greatest and most important actor in the history of the cinema.[[134]](#cite_note-schwarz20070102-151)[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann19974McBride201385-421) He was a favorite of Hitchcock's, who admired him and called him "the only actor I ever loved in my whole life".[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann19973-422) He remained one of Hollywood's top box-office attractions for almost 30 years.[[395]](#cite_note-423) [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") stated that the world still thinks of him affectionately because he "embodies what seems a happier time−a time when we had a simpler relationship to a performer".[[102]](#cite_note-Kael-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Cary_Grant_Statue.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_Statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Statue.jpg)

*Cary Grant* statue by [Graham Ibbeson](/wiki/Graham_Ibbeson "Graham Ibbeson") (2001) in [Millennium Square](/wiki/Millennium_Square_(Bristol) "Millennium Square (Bristol)"), Bristol

Grant was nominated for Academy Awards for *[Penny Serenade](/wiki/Penny_Serenade "Penny Serenade")* (1941) and *[None But the Lonely Heart](/wiki/None_But_the_Lonely_Heart_(film) "None But the Lonely Heart (film)")* (1944),[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouse200599-424) but he never won a competitive Oscar.[[ac]](#cite_note-426)[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERingler2000182-427) He did, however, receive a special [Academy Award for Lifetime Achievement](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") in 1970.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEProno2008127-236) The inscription on his statuette read "To Cary Grant, for his unique mastery of the art of screen acting with respect and affection of his colleagues". Presenting the award to Grant, [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") announced: "No one has brought more pleasure to more people for so many years than Cary has, and nobody has done so many things so well".[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997246–247-428)

Grant was awarded a special plaque at the Straw Hat Awards in New York in May 1975, which recognized him as a "star and superstar in entertainment". In July 1976, [Betty Ford](/wiki/Betty_Ford "Betty Ford") invited him to attend the Bicentennial dinner for Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") and to give a speech introducing her at the [Republican National Convention](/wiki/Republican_National_Convention "Republican National Convention") in [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") the following month. He was invited to a royal charity gala in 1978 at the [London Palladium](/wiki/London_Palladium "London Palladium"). In 1979, he hosted the American Film Institute's tribute to Alfred Hitchcock, and presented [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier") with his honorary Oscar.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001300-429)

In 1981, Grant was accorded the [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors").[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClear199380-430) In 1984, a theater on the MGM lot was renamed the "Cary Grant Theatre".[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997265-295) In 1995, more than 100 leading film directors were asked to reveal their favorite actor of all time in a *Time Out* poll, and Grant came second only to [Marlon Brando](/wiki/Marlon_Brando "Marlon Brando").[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell20118-431) In December 2001, a statue of Grant by [Graham Ibbeson](/wiki/Graham_Ibbeson "Graham Ibbeson") was unveiled in [Millennium Square](/wiki/Millennium_Square_(Bristol) "Millennium Square (Bristol)"), a regenerated area next to [Bristol Harbour](/wiki/Bristol_Harbour "Bristol Harbour"), Bristol, the city where he was born.[[403]](#cite_note-432) In November 2005, Grant again came first in *Premiere* magazine's list of "The 50 Greatest Movie Stars of All Time".[[404]](#cite_note-433) The biennial Cary Comes Home Festival was established in 2014 in his hometown [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol").[[405]](#cite_note-434) McCann declared that Grant was "quite simply, the funniest actor cinema has ever produced".[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199735-435)

There is a street named after Grant in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), Texas.[[407]](#cite_note-436)

In November 2024, a [Historic England](/wiki/Historic_England "Historic England") national [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") was unveiled on his childhood home at 50 Berkeley Road, [Bishopston, Bristol](/wiki/Bishopston,_Bristol "Bishopston, Bristol"), where he lived from about age four to six, by Heritage Minister [Sir Chris Bryant](/wiki/Sir_Chris_Bryant "Sir Chris Bryant").[[23]](#cite_note-bp-20241122-25)[[408]](#cite_note-437)

### Portrayals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Portrayals")]

Grant was portrayed by [John Gavin](/wiki/John_Gavin "John Gavin") in the 1980 made-for-television biographical film *[Sophia Loren: Her Own Story](/wiki/Sophia_Loren:_Her_Own_Story "Sophia Loren: Her Own Story")*,[[409]](#cite_note-438) and in the similar 1987 TV serialisation *[Poor Little Rich Girl: The Barbara Hutton Story](/wiki/Poor_Little_Rich_Girl:_The_Barbara_Hutton_Story "Poor Little Rich Girl: The Barbara Hutton Story")*, [James Read](/wiki/James_Read "James Read") played Grant as Barbara Hutton's third husband.

The British miniseries *[Archie](/wiki/Archie_(miniseries) "Archie (miniseries)")*, covering Grant's life, was broadcast from November 2023 on [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)"). Grant is portrayed by Dainton Anderson, [Oaklee Pendergast](/wiki/Oaklee_Pendergast "Oaklee Pendergast"), [Calam Lynch](/wiki/Calam_Lynch "Calam Lynch") and [Jason Isaacs](/wiki/Jason_Isaacs "Jason Isaacs") at successive stages of his life.[[410]](#cite_note-439)

## Filmography and stage work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Filmography and stage work")]

Main article: [List of Cary Grant performances](/wiki/List_of_Cary_Grant_performances "List of Cary Grant performances")

From 1932 to 1966, Grant starred in over seventy films. In 1999, the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") named him the second-greatest male star of [Golden Age Hollywood](/wiki/Classical_Hollywood_cinema "Classical Hollywood cinema") cinema (after [Humphrey Bogart](/wiki/Humphrey_Bogart "Humphrey Bogart")).[[411]](#cite_note-440) He was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for *[Penny Serenade](/wiki/Penny_Serenade "Penny Serenade")* (1941) and *[None but the Lonely Heart](/wiki/None_but_the_Lonely_Heart_(film) "None but the Lonely Heart (film)")* (1944).[[178]](#cite_note-CG44-198)[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrouse200599Wansell2011120-441)

Widely recognized for comedic and dramatic roles, among his best-known films are:[[8]](#cite_note-ATCF-9)

* *[Blonde Venus](/wiki/Blonde_Venus "Blonde Venus")* (1932) with [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich")
* *[She Done Him Wrong](/wiki/She_Done_Him_Wrong "She Done Him Wrong")* (1933) with [Mae West](/wiki/Mae_West "Mae West")
* *[Sylvia Scarlett](/wiki/Sylvia_Scarlett "Sylvia Scarlett")* (1935) with [Katharine Hepburn](/wiki/Katharine_Hepburn "Katharine Hepburn")
* *[The Awful Truth](/wiki/The_Awful_Truth "The Awful Truth")* (1937) with [Irene Dunne](/wiki/Irene_Dunne "Irene Dunne")
* *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")* (1938) with Katharine Hepburn
* *[Holiday](/wiki/Holiday_(1938_film) "Holiday (1938 film)")* (1938) with Katharine Hepburn
* *[Gunga Din](/wiki/Gunga_Din_(film) "Gunga Din (film)")* (1939) with [Victor McLaglen](/wiki/Victor_McLaglen "Victor McLaglen") and [Douglas Fairbanks Jr.](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks_Jr. "Douglas Fairbanks Jr.")
* *[Only Angels Have Wings](/wiki/Only_Angels_Have_Wings "Only Angels Have Wings")* (1939) with [Jean Arthur](/wiki/Jean_Arthur "Jean Arthur") and [Rita Hayworth](/wiki/Rita_Hayworth "Rita Hayworth")
* *[His Girl Friday](/wiki/His_Girl_Friday "His Girl Friday")* (1940) with [Rosalind Russell](/wiki/Rosalind_Russell "Rosalind Russell")
* *[My Favorite Wife](/wiki/My_Favorite_Wife "My Favorite Wife")* (1940) with Irene Dunne
* *[The Philadelphia Story](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Story_(film) "The Philadelphia Story (film)")* (1940) with Katharine Hepburn and [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart")
* *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941) with [Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine")
* *[Arsenic and Old Lace](/wiki/Arsenic_and_Old_Lace_(film) "Arsenic and Old Lace (film)")* (1944) with [Priscilla Lane](/wiki/Priscilla_Lane "Priscilla Lane") and [Peter Lorre](/wiki/Peter_Lorre "Peter Lorre")
* *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946) with [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman")
* *[The Bishop's Wife](/wiki/The_Bishop%27s_Wife "The Bishop's Wife")* (1947) with [Loretta Young](/wiki/Loretta_Young "Loretta Young")
* *[Monkey Business](/wiki/Monkey_Business_(1952_film) "Monkey Business (1952 film)")* (1952) with [Ginger Rogers](/wiki/Ginger_Rogers "Ginger Rogers") and [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe")
* *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")* (1955) with [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly")
* *[An Affair to Remember](/wiki/An_Affair_to_Remember "An Affair to Remember")* (1957) with [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr")
* *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959) with [Eva Marie Saint](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint") and [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason")
* *[Charade](/wiki/Charade_(1963_film) "Charade (1963 film)")* (1963) with [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cary_Grant&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-MiddleName_4-0)** His middle name was recorded as "Alec" on birth records, although he used "Alexander" on his naturalization application form in 1942.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot2004390-1)[[2]](#cite_note-BirthRecordList-2)[[3]](#cite_note-McCarthy-3)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Among the reasons that he gave for believing so was that he was circumcised, and [circumcision](/wiki/Circumcision "Circumcision") was and still is rare in Britain outside the Jewish community.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199714–15-15) In 1948, he donated a large sum of money to help the newly established State of Israel, declaring that it was "in the name of his dead Jewish mother".[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2004114-16) He also speculated that his appearance, with brown curly hair, could be due to his father's partly Jewish descent. There is no genealogical or substantial evidence about possible Jewish ancestry, however.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199716-17) He turned down the leading role in *[Gentleman's Agreement](/wiki/Gentleman%27s_Agreement "Gentleman's Agreement")* in the 1940s, playing a non-Jewish character who pretends to be Jewish, because he believed that he could not effectively play the part. He donated considerable sums to Jewish causes over his lifetime. In 1939, he gave Jewish actor [Sam Jaffe](/wiki/Sam_Jaffe "Sam Jaffe") $25,000.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHighamMoseley19903McCann199714–15-18)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** Wansell states that John was a "sickly child" who frequently came down with a fever. He had developed gangrene on his arms after a door was slammed on his thumbnail while his mother was holding him. She stayed up night after night nursing him, but the doctor insisted that she get some rest—and he died the night that she stopped watching over him.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201113-11)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-42)** Wansell notes that Grant hated mathematics and Latin and was more interested in geography, because he "wanted to travel".[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201116-41)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-69)** Grant likely made further changes to his accent after electing to remain in the United States, in an effort to make himself more employable.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199759–60-67) The slight Cockney accent that Grant had picked up during his time with the Pender troupe, blended with his efforts to sound American, resulted in his unique manner of speaking.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson200255–56-68)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-75)** The play's success prompted a screen test for Grant and MacDonald by Paramount Publix Pictures at [Astoria Studios](/wiki/Astoria_Studios "Astoria Studios") in New York, which resulted in MacDonald being cast opposite [Maurice Chevalier](/wiki/Maurice_Chevalier "Maurice Chevalier") in *[The Love Parade](/wiki/The_Love_Parade "The Love Parade")* (1929). Grant was rejected, and informed that his neck was "too thick" and his legs were "too bowed".[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201120-72)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** The productions included *[Irene](/wiki/Irene_(musical) "Irene (musical)")*, *Music in May*, *Nina Rosa*, *[Rio Rita](/wiki/Rio_Rita_(musical) "Rio Rita (musical)")*, and *[The Three Musketeers](/wiki/The_Three_Musketeers_(musical) "The Three Musketeers (musical)")*.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBottoViagas2010493Wansell201121-82)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-88)** Grant was later so embarrassed by the scene and he requested that it be omitted from his 1970 Academy Award footage.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199757-87)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-90)** Grant later worked with Gering in *[Devil and the Deep](/wiki/Devil_and_the_Deep "Devil and the Deep")* and *[Madame Butterfly](/wiki/Madame_Butterfly_(1932_film) "Madame Butterfly (1932 film)")* (both 1932).
10. **[^](#cite_ref-95)** Grant agreed that "Archie just doesn't sound right in America. It doesn't sound particularly right in Britain either".[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann199761-94) While having dinner with Fay Wray, she suggested that he choose "Cary Lockwood", the name of his character in *Nikki*. Schulberg agreed the name "Cary" was acceptable, but was less satisfied with "Lockwood" as it was too similar to another actor's surname. Schulberg then gave Grant a list of surnames compiled by Paramount's publicity department, out of which he chose "Grant".[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200457-93)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-109)** *She Done Him Wrong*—an adaptation of Mae West's own play *[Diamond Lil](/wiki/Diamond_Lil_(play) "Diamond Lil (play)")* (1928)—was nominated in the [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") category, but lost to *[Cavalcade](/wiki/Cavalcade_(1933_film) "Cavalcade (1933 film)")* (1933).[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200468–69-107)[[98]](#cite_note-108)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-112)** According to biographer Jerry Vermilye, Grant had caught West's eye in the studio and had queried about him to one of Paramount's office boys. The boy replied, "Oh, that's Cary Grant. He's making [*Madame*] *Butterfly* with Sylvia Sidney". West then retorted, "I don't care if he's making Little Nell. If he can talk, I'll take him."[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye197330-111)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-118)** The film is ranked at 75 in [AFI's 100 Years... 100 Laughs](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years..._100_Laughs "AFI's 100 Years... 100 Laughs") list, while West's line "Why don't you come up sometime and see me?" was voted number 26 in [AFI's 100 Years... 100 Movie Quotes](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years..._100_Movie_Quotes "AFI's 100 Years... 100 Movie Quotes").[[104]](#cite_note-AFI_Laughs-116)[[105]](#cite_note-117)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-122)** *The New York Times* called *Born to Be Bad* a "hopelessly unintelligent hodgepodge", while *Variety* labelled his performance "colorless" and "meaningless".[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201136-121)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-125)** In December 1934 Virginia Cherrill informed a jury in a Los Angeles court that Grant "drank excessively, choked and beat her, and threatened to kill her". The press continued to report on the turbulent relationship which began to tarnish his image.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201138-124)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-128)** Though Grant's films in the 1934–1935 period were commercial failures, he was still getting positive comments from the critics, who thought that his acting was getting better. One reviewer from *Daily Variety* wrote of *Wings in the Dark*: "Cary Grant tops all his past work. The part gave him a dimension to play with and he took it headlong. He never flaws in the moving, pathetic, but inspiring behavior of a man whose career seems ruined by an accident but comes back through a mental hell, by virtue of love and the saving ruses of friendship. His acting here lifts him definitely above his prior standing."[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner197384-126) Graham Greene of *The Spectator* thought that he played his role in *The Last Outpost* "extremely well".[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner197386-127)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-133)** The pair later featured in *[Bringing Up Baby](/wiki/Bringing_Up_Baby "Bringing Up Baby")* (1938), *[Holiday](/wiki/Holiday_(1938_film) "Holiday (1938 film)")* (1938) and *[The Philadelphia Story](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Story_(film) "The Philadelphia Story (film)")* (1940).[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermilye1973146–148-132)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-162)** The film was actually shot at [Lone Pine, California](/wiki/Lone_Pine,_California "Lone Pine, California") in one of the largest sets ever assembled, with over 1,500 extras.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWansell201154-161)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-168)** *His Girl Friday* is ranked number 19 on [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute")'s [100 Years...100 Laughs](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Laughs "AFI's 100 Years...100 Laughs") and number 13 on *The Guardian*'s list of the greatest comedy films of all time, compiled in 2010.[[104]](#cite_note-AFI_Laughs-116)[[149]](#cite_note-Guardian_HGF-167)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-172)** *Time* claim that Grant himself earned $100,000 for the film.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeschner1973143-171)
21. **[^](#cite_ref-230)** Critical response to the film at the time was mixed. [Bosley Crowther](/wiki/Bosley_Crowther "Bosley Crowther") wrote: "It is simply a concoction of crazy, fast, uninhibited farce. This sort of thing, when done well—as it generally is, in this case—can be insanely funny (if it hits right). It can also be a bore."[[209]](#cite_note-229)
22. **[^](#cite_ref-235)** Grant also continued to find the experience of working with Hitchcock a positive one, remarking: "Hitch and I had a rapport and understanding deeper than words. He was a very agreeable human being, and we were very compatible ... Nothing ever went wrong. He was so incredibly well prepared. I never know anyone as capable".[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann1997214-233)
23. **[^](#cite_ref-244)** Loren later professed about rejecting Grant: "At the time I didn't have any regrets, I was in love with my husband. I was very affectionate with Cary, but I was 23 years old. I couldn't make up my mind to marry a giant from another country and leave Carlo. I didn't feel like making the big step."[[221]](#cite_note-Thorpe-243)
24. **[^](#cite_ref-255)** *North by Northwest* is placed at the 41st position on AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies,[[229]](#cite_note-252) 7th on its 100 Years...100 Thrills list,[[230]](#cite_note-253) and was voted the seventh-greatest [mystery film](/wiki/Mystery_film "Mystery film") in its 10 Top 10 mystery films list.[[231]](#cite_note-254)
25. **[^](#cite_ref-293)** [Prince Rainier of Monaco](/wiki/Prince_Rainier_of_Monaco "Prince Rainier of Monaco"), Kelly's widower, said: "Grace loved and admired Cary. She valued his friendship".[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorecambeSterling2001319-292)
26. **[^](#cite_ref-358)** Grant was quoted as saying: "I may not have married for very sound reasons, but money was never one of them."[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHadleigh2012212-357)
27. **[^](#cite_ref-377)** Grant had a reputation for filing lawsuits against the film industry since the 1930s. The basis of these suits was that he had been cheated by the respective company. Most were described as frivolous and were settled out of court. A proposal was made to present him with an Academy Honorary Award in 1969; it was vetoed by angry Academy members. The proposal garnered enough votes to pass in 1970. It is believed[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] that Bouron's accusations were part of a smear campaign organized by those in the film industry.[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEliot200413–19-376)
28. **[^](#cite_ref-381)** In 1973, Bouron was found murdered in a San Fernando parking lot.[[353]](#cite_note-380)
29. **[^](#cite_ref-426)** Jennifer Grant states that her father was quite outspoken on the discrimination that he felt against handsome men and comedians in Hollywood. He questioned "are good looks their own reward, canceling out the right to more"? She recalls that he once said of [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford"): "It'll be tough for him to be awarded anything, he's just too good looking".[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrant201168-425)
